proposals.title: Revisiting the NOW Architecture for Moderate-Communication Workloads: A Case Study in Astronomical Image Processing
proposals.motivation: The Network of Workstations (NOW) model, pioneered by Patterson and others, has been highly successful for embarrassingly parallel problems but is often considered inefficient for tasks requiring frequent inter-node communication. However, the rise of cost-effective, high-bandwidth interconnects (e.g., RDMA over Converged Ethernet) and in-memory data frameworks (e.g., Apache Spark) challenges this assumption. Many modern data-intensive workloads, like the astronomical image processing in the Kira project, exhibit moderate, structured communication patterns that may now be viable on commodity clusters.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a modern NOW, leveraging contemporary cloud hardware, caching layers, and data-aware task scheduling, can execute moderate-communication graph and imaging workloads with efficiency rivaling specialized HPC systems, fundamentally expanding the cost-effective applicability of the NOW model.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira astronomy image processing pipeline as a benchmark, instrumenting it to model communication graphs. We will design and implement a dedicated cache-aware scheduler within a framework like Spark, optimizing for data locality and minimizing serialization overhead. The system will be deployed on a cloud-based NOW testbed using instances with varied network capabilities (standard vs. RDMA-enabled). We will compare performance and cost against a baseline implementation on a traditional, tightly-coupled small HPC cluster.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, data-driven framework for evaluating the communication-efficiency boundary of modern NOWs. It will deliver an open-source, optimized scheduler for moderate-communication workloads and concrete architectural guidelines for when a commodity cluster is preferable to specialized hardware for data-intensive scientific computing.
proposals.novelty: While NOWs are well-studied for high- and low-communication extremes, this work systematically targets the under-explored 'moderate' regime using pre-2026 but cutting-edge cloud hardware and software stacks, directly testing a long-held architectural assumption with a real-world, data-intensive application.

proposals.title: Global-NOW: An Architecture for Internet-Scale Desktop Grid Computing with Volunteer and Commercial Resources
proposals.motivation: The Network of Workstations (NOW) concept demonstrated the power of commodity clusters, but its scale was limited to institutional boundaries. Meanwhile, projects like SETI@home proved the vast, untapped potential of global volunteer computing, yet they remain specialized, application-specific silos. There is a critical need for a general-purpose, global-scale parallel computing architecture that can harness idle cycles from billions of desktops and edge devices, creating a sustainable alternative to centralized cloud expansion for data-intensive workloads like astronomy imagery analysis (Kira) or genomics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating principles from the NOW architecture, modern big data frameworks (e.g., Apache Spark), and secure, incentive-aligned resource markets, we can design a viable, fault-tolerant global desktop grid that efficiently executes general-purpose parallel applications.
proposals.method: We will design a layered architecture comprising: 1) a secure, sandboxed execution environment using WebAssembly (Wasm) for safe, portable compute capsules, 2) a decentralized, fault-tolerant task scheduler inspired by Spark's Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs) but optimized for high churn and heterogeneity, 3) a hybrid incentive layer combining 'proof-of-useful-work' reputation for volunteers and a lightweight micropayment channel (inspired by blockchain state channels, pre-2026) for commercial resource trading. We will prototype and evaluate using the Kira astronomy pipeline and genomic sequence alignment as benchmark workloads.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel systems architecture for global-scale, general-purpose desktop grids, a set of algorithms for fault-tolerant scheduling in highly volatile environments, and an empirical evaluation of economic and technical incentives for resource contribution. It extends Patterson's legacy of commodity cluster computing to an internet-wide scale.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing the NOW's general-purpose parallel computing model with the global scale of volunteer computing, while introducing a hybrid incentive model and secure Wasm-based sandboxing to overcome key adoption barriers of trust, security, and sustainability that plagued earlier efforts.

proposals.title: DUAL: Emulating Tightly-Coupled Supercomputer Performance on a Network of Workstations
proposals.motivation: The cost and physical constraints of dedicated supercomputers limit access for algorithm development and architectural exploration. Meanwhile, Networks of Workstations (NOWs), built from commodity cloud or on-premise hardware, offer immense scale but are optimized for loosely-coupled, data-parallel workloads. There is a critical gap in understanding if and how a large NOW can faithfully emulate the performance characteristics—particularly low-latency communication and synchronized execution—of a tightly-coupled system for HPC-class applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by implementing a lightweight, specialized runtime system that virtualizes supercomputer node interconnects and carefully manages cache-coherent memory regions across the NOW, we can emulate the performance profile of a tightly-coupled supercomputer for a defined class of iterative graph and mesh-based computations.
proposals.method: We will extend the design principles of the earlier NOW project and the Kira astronomy pipeline to build the DUAL (Distributed Unified Architecture Layer) emulation framework. DUAL will use a partitioned global address space (PGAS) model, implemented via a user-level runtime that leverages RDMA over converged Ethernet (RoCE) on modern cloud instances to simulate low-latency interconnects like InfiniBand. We will develop a performance model and trace-driven simulator, validated against real supercomputer logs (e.g., from NERSC), to guide the emulation. Key applications for validation will include iterative graph algorithms (e.g., PageRank, breadth-first search) and structured grid simulations, mirroring the computational patterns in astronomy imagery processing.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available software framework for cost-effective supercomputer performance emulation, a detailed quantitative analysis of the fidelity and limitations of NOW-based emulation, and a set of architectural insights into the fundamental bottlenecks when decoupling communication hardware from application logic. It will provide a new methodology for pre-silicon and pre-deployment software/hardware co-design.
proposals.novelty: While NOWs are used for distributed computing, using them to *emulate* (not just execute) the specific performance behavior of a different, tightly-coupled architecture is novel. The approach integrates Patterson's expertise in scalable systems (NOW) with modern cloud hardware (RDMA) and specific application domains (graph, Kira) to solve a distinct performance modeling problem.

proposals.title: Bimodal System Architecture for Predictable Baselines with Managed Burst Regimes: A Framework for Cost-Performance Optimization
proposals.motivation: Modern cloud and data-intensive systems, such as those for genomic or astronomical (e.g., Kira) processing, face a fundamental tension. Over-provisioning for peak load is cost-prohibitive, while under-provisioning leads to unpredictable latency and poor user experience. Existing auto-scaling solutions often introduce significant latency variance and complexity, failing to provide the predictable baselines required for interactive or latency-sensitive applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A formally partitioned bimodal architecture, with a tightly optimized core for baseline load and a separately provisioned, potentially heterogeneous burst layer, can provide superior and more predictable cost-performance trade-offs compared to monolithic or purely elastic designs for workloads with known, variable operating envelopes.
proposals.method: We will develop a formal framework modeling the trade-offs in latency, cost, and consistency. This will be validated through simulation and prototype implementation. The core tier will leverage predictable hardware (e.g., custom chips, cache-optimized data structures) for common-case operations. The burst tier will employ scalable but potentially higher-latency big data technologies (e.g., Spark-like processing) with relaxed consistency. We will design a dynamic load director, informed by the framework, to manage regime transitions and data synchronization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a set of principled design guidelines and a quantitative evaluation framework for architects building systems with variable but bounded load. It will deliver concrete architectural patterns and a prototype demonstrating how to achieve robust, predictable performance without assuming infinite scalability.
proposals.novelty: While tiered systems exist, the novelty lies in the formal co-design of two architecturally distinct regimes—one for predictable baselines and one for managed bursts—with explicit trade-off analysis. This moves beyond simple scaling to a principled, heterogeneous system partitioning problem.

proposals.title: Bounded-Intensity Serverless: A Predictable Compute Fabric for Millisecond-Scale Task Graphs
proposals.motivation: Current serverless platforms (e.g., AWS Lambda, Azure Functions) excel at scaling unpredictable, high-latency-tolerant workloads but fail to provide predictable performance for time-sensitive, fine-grained task graphs. Emerging applications in edge analytics, high-frequency trading, and real-time sensor processing require millisecond-scale orchestration with strong tail-latency guarantees, which the scale-out, best-effort utility model cannot satisfy. This gap prevents serverless from becoming a viable platform for deterministic soft real-time systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling and enforcing finite platform capacity as a first-class constraint, a serverless system can provide a stable, predictable performance envelope, enabling reliable orchestration of complex task graphs with millisecond-scale tasks and bounded tail latency.
proposals.method: We will design a new serverless architecture with a centralized, low-latency scheduler co-located with compute nodes on a single chip or tightly-coupled rack, inspired by warehouse-scale computer principles. The system will employ a rate-based admission controller and a deterministic, priority-aware task scheduler (e.g., an EDF variant) to enforce bounded intensity. We will implement a lightweight, RDMA-based communication layer for inter-task state access and evaluate the prototype using task graphs derived from astronomy image processing (Kira) and genomic pipeline workloads, measuring tail latency and throughput stability under controlled load.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new serverless execution model that provides predictable performance, making serverless viable for a new class of soft real-time applications. It will contribute a novel scheduling and resource provisioning framework that treats capacity as a guarantee rather than an optimization target, alongside an open-source prototype demonstrating millisecond-scale task orchestration.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in rejecting the infinite-scale, best-effort paradigm of mainstream serverless in favor of a bounded-intensity design that prioritizes predictability, applying principles from real-time and embedded systems to the cloud-native serverless abstraction.

proposals.title: Kira-Cloud: Declarative, Capability-Aware Scheduling for Heterogeneous Astronomical Image Processing
proposals.motivation: Cloud infrastructure is increasingly heterogeneous, incorporating GPUs, FPGAs, and specialized ASICs (e.g., TPUs, IPUs). However, this diversity burdens developers with complex, low-level hardware selection and orchestration, negating the cloud's promise of simplicity. This problem is acute in data-intensive fields like astronomy, where projects like the LSST generate petabyte-scale datasets requiring efficient, cost-effective processing pipelines, as seen in the original Kira work.
proposals.hypothesis: A cloud management system that uses a declarative, intent-based API coupled with a reinforcement learning scheduler can automatically and optimally map high-level workload specifications to heterogeneous hardware, delivering superior performance and cost efficiency compared to both static mappings and user-specified placements.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira big-data astronomy processing framework with a new scheduling layer. The system will expose a declarative API for users to specify constraints (deadline, budget, accuracy). A central scheduler, modeled as a contextual multi-armed bandit problem, will use real-time telemetry (performance, power, cost) and a learned cost-performance model to map workloads to hardware instances (e.g., GPU clusters vs. FPGA-enabled VMs). We will implement and evaluate this on a private cloud testbed featuring diverse hardware, using LSST image analysis pipelines as our primary benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully functional, open-source prototype system that demonstrably lowers the barrier to using heterogeneous cloud hardware. It will provide a quantitative analysis of the performance-cost trade-offs enabled by automated scheduling and a novel methodology for treating hardware capabilities as a learnable resource for cloud schedulers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on scheduling for homogeneous clusters or GPU-only workloads, this integrates the multi-armed bandit approach—common in web advertising—directly into cloud resource management for diverse ASICs/FPGAs. It uniquely combines Patterson's experience in architecture (RISC-V, hardware specialization) and big-data systems (Kira) to tackle the developer abstraction problem for next-generation cloud infrastructure.

proposals.title: Metabolic Cloud: A Pathway-Inspired Orchestrator for Heterogeneous Hardware
proposals.motivation: Modern cloud infrastructure is increasingly heterogeneous, incorporating CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs, and emerging accelerators like TPUs, but existing orchestration systems (e.g., Kubernetes) treat these as uniform resources with simple labels, leading to suboptimal placement and utilization. This inefficiency wastes energy, increases cost, and fails to meet the performance demands of diverse workloads like genomics, astronomy (e.g., Kira), and graph analytics. A new paradigm is needed to dynamically and intelligently map complex computational tasks onto a network of specialized hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling a heterogeneous cloud as a network of catalytic resources and computational tasks as substrates, analogous to metabolic pathways in systems biology, will enable an orchestrator that dynamically optimizes resource allocation for superior performance-per-watt and cost-efficiency compared to state-of-the-art schedulers.
proposals.method: We will develop a graph-based model where nodes represent heterogeneous hardware units (characterized by performance, power, and cost profiles via microbenchmarks) and edges represent data transfer pathways. Incoming workloads will be profiled and decomposed into task graphs. The core scheduling algorithm will adapt Flux Balance Analysis (FBA) from metabolic network optimization, treating hardware as enzymes and tasks as substrates to compute an optimal 'flux' of work through the resource graph. This will be integrated as a custom scheduler for Kubernetes, leveraging prior work on hardware telemetry and the RISC-V ecosystem for agile hardware experimentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, biologically-inspired formal model and scheduling algorithm for heterogeneous clouds, 2) an open-source prototype orchestrator, 'Metabolic Cloud', and 3) a quantitative evaluation demonstrating significant improvements in performance-per-watt and cost for benchmark suites including genomics and astronomy imaging pipelines on a testbed featuring CPUs, GPUs, and FPGAs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the rigorous optimization framework of metabolic pathway analysis (FBA) to cloud resource orchestration, moving beyond simple bin-packing or heuristic scheduling to a holistic, network-flow-based optimization of heterogeneous computational flux.

proposals.title: Kira-Fabric: A Federated, Hardware-Aware Scheduler for Astronomy and Genomic Workflows Across Heterogeneous Clouds
proposals.motivation: The rise of specialized hardware (GPUs, TPUs, FPGAs) for AI, graph processing, and genomics creates a fragmented cloud market where no single provider offers the optimal mix for all workloads. Applications like the Kira astronomy pipeline or genomic variant calling must currently be locked into a single cloud or manually partitioned, sacrificing performance and cost efficiency. A federated fabric that treats diverse cloud hardware as a single, composable resource is the next logical step in cloud architecture, enabling true vertical specialization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated scheduler, using a unified abstraction layer for heterogeneous accelerators and a cache-aware performance model, can dynamically compose optimal virtual clusters from multiple clouds, reducing total cost of computation and time-to-solution for data-intensive workloads compared to single-provider deployment.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira/Big Data technology stack with a new federated orchestration layer. This involves: 1) Developing a 'Hardware Capability Profile' (HCP) schema, extending ideas from cache-aware scheduling and chip architecture, to abstract GPU/TPU/FPGA/CPU resources across providers. 2) Designing a two-tier scheduler: a global federation manager using a modified Heterogeneous Earliest Finish Time (HEFT) algorithm for inter-cloud placement, and local agents for intra-cloud provisioning. 3) Implementing a performance prediction model based on historical job data and hardware profiles to guide placement, optimizing for data locality and accelerator specialization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical architecture and open-source prototype for federated, hardware-aware cloud scheduling. It will provide a quantitative framework for evaluating the trade-offs (performance, cost, energy) of vertical specialization across administrative domains. The results will advance the field beyond monolithic clouds and simple load balancing towards intelligent, economic-driven resource composition.
proposals.novelty: While multi-cloud and hybrid cloud exist, prior work focuses on redundancy or aggregating generic VMs. Our approach is novel in its focus on dynamic, fine-grained composition of *specialized* accelerator resources from competing providers into a single logical fabric, driven by workload-specific performance models derived from big data processing principles.

proposals.title: Kira-Cost: A Unified, Dynamic Cost-Optimization Framework for Data-Intensive Astronomy Pipelines in the Cloud
proposals.motivation: Modern cloud-based scientific computing, exemplified by projects like Kira for astronomy imagery, faces a critical cost-management challenge. Current optimization strategies often focus myopically on a single primary cost driver—be it computation, storage, or egress—leading to sub-optimal total system expenditure as cloud spot prices and workload patterns fluctuate. A holistic model that dynamically trades off these interdependent costs is needed to make large-scale data processing sustainably affordable for research institutions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fine-grained, adaptable cost model, coupled with an intelligent orchestrator that makes continuous decisions on data locality, computational redundancy, and caching, can minimize total system cost for data-intensive workloads without a priori bias toward any single expense category.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira architecture with a cost-orchestration layer. This layer will integrate a dynamic cost model using real-time telemetry from cloud APIs (e.g., AWS Spot pricing, S3/EFS costs) and workload monitors. The orchestrator will employ a lightweight, online optimization algorithm (e.g., a reinforcement learning agent or a predictive model-based controller) to decide per-processing-stage actions: whether to recompute, cache intermediate data on fast local SSDs, or pay for repeated data transfers from object storage. We will prototype this on a scaled-down astronomy dataset using cloud instances with varied storage and network hardware profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, unified cost-optimization framework and a concrete, open-source implementation for the Kira ecosystem. It will provide the community with a methodology and quantitative results demonstrating significant cost savings over static, single-driver optimization policies for variable cloud-based scientific workloads.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that statically prioritizes computation or data transfer, our framework dynamically models their trade-off in response to real-time price signals and workload characteristics, enabling automatic, context-aware cost minimization for complex pipelines.

proposals.title: The Dark Energy and Dark Matter of Distributed Systems: A DTDM Framework for Architectural and Economic Analysis
proposals.motivation: The cost models for cloud and high-performance computing remain overwhelmingly compute-centric, focusing on CPU/GPU cycles and FLOPs. Yet, in data-intensive domains like astronomy (e.g., Kira), genomics, and graph analytics, the systemic costs of data movement across networks, memory hierarchies, and storage layers often dominate total expenditure and latency. This misalignment leads to inefficient architectural choices and inaccurate total cost of ownership (TCO) predictions for large-scale systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that data transfer (dynamic, energy-like cost) and data persistence/state (inertial, matter-like cost) are the primary, often hidden, cost drivers in modern distributed systems, and that a paradigm shift in measurement and modeling—treating these as first-class economic and performance factors—will yield more accurate benchmarks and more cost-effective architectures.
proposals.method: We will develop the Data-Transfer-Dark-Matter (DTDM) framework by: 1) Instrumenting existing big data stacks (e.g., Apache Spark, used in Kira) and cloud workloads to collect fine-grained telemetry on data movement across cache hierarchies, node interconnects, and storage tiers. 2) Creating new benchmark suites and cost models that weight data movement and state maintenance metrics (e.g., byte-miles, stateful operation duration) equally with compute operations. 3) Applying this framework to analyze and propose optimizations for specific architectures, including novel near-memory processing chips and data-centric network topologies, to validate cost savings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A new benchmarking methodology and open-source tools for quantifying the 'dark costs' of distributed systems. 2) Revised architectural guidelines and economic models for cloud providers and hardware designers, shifting focus from pure compute efficiency to total data movement efficiency. 3) A concrete analysis of how this framework changes the evaluation of systems for Patterson's domains of astronomy imagery and genomic processing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the cosmological analogy of dark energy/dark matter as a rigorous conceptual framework to challenge the compute-centric dogma in systems research, moving beyond acknowledging data movement as a problem to making it the central lens for cost and performance analysis.

proposals.title: Kira-Cost: Fine-Grained Cost Attribution and Optimization for Astronomy Data Pipelines Using Annotated Lineage Graphs
proposals.motivation: The shift of data-intensive scientific workflows, like the Kira astronomy image processing pipeline, to public cloud infrastructure has exposed a critical gap: while lineage systems track data provenance, they lack integration with the nuanced cost models of cloud storage (e.g., S3 tiers) and data transfer (inter-region egress). This makes the financial impact of pipeline design decisions—such as materializing intermediate datasets or choosing storage classes—opaque, leading to unexpectedly high operational costs that can constrain scientific throughput.
proposals.hypothesis: By instrumenting a scientific workflow engine (like Apache Spark) to capture fine-grained data lineage and annotating the resulting provenance graph with actual cloud provider pricing for storage operations and data movement, we can accurately attribute costs to pipeline steps and enable automated identification of high-cost, low-value data artifacts for optimization.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira/Spark architecture to instrument tasks, capturing lineage for RDDs/DataFrames. A cost annotation engine will integrate real-time pricing APIs from major cloud providers (AWS, GCP) to tag lineage nodes and edges with costs for storage class, duration, and network transfer. We will develop a graph analysis library to propagate costs through the DAG, attribute them to user code, and implement heuristic algorithms (inspired by cache eviction policies) to recommend changes, such as deleting specific intermediate checkpoints, altering storage classes, or co-locating compute. The system will be validated using real Kira workloads on cloud infrastructure.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source framework for cost-aware data lineage, moving beyond performance debugging to economic debugging. It will provide a methodology and tool for scientists and engineers to make storage and transfer costs a first-class, actionable constraint in pipeline design, directly reducing the operational expense of large-scale scientific computing in the cloud.
proposals.novelty: Prior work on data lineage (e.g., Titian) and cloud cost management are largely separate domains. This research fuses them by treating the lineage graph as a cost-accounting structure, enabling fine-grained, retrospective cost attribution and proactive optimization recommendations specific to scientific workflow DAGs, which is not addressed by generic cloud billing tools.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Cost Bottleneck in Data-Intensive Pipelines: A Case Study in Astronomy and Genomics
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in high-performance and cloud computing, championed by trends like Moore's Law and specialized accelerators, assumes computation is the primary cost and optimization target. However, for data-intensive fields like astronomy (e.g., Kira pipeline) and genomics, the volume of data is exploding, making movement and persistent storage increasingly expensive. This proposal challenges the computational focus by quantifying the often-overlooked total cost of ownership (TCO) for data-centric workflows in modern, disaggregated cloud architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: For large-scale, data-intensive scientific and commercial pipelines, the cumulative costs of data transfer (ingress/egress, inter-node communication) and long-term storage will dominate the total system cost, making computational efficiency a secondary concern in overall economic and energy models.
proposals.method: We will instrument existing pipelines, including the Kira astronomy image processing stack and genomic variant calling workflows, to create detailed cost models. Using performance counters and cloud billing APIs, we will profile real monetary and energy costs for computation (vCPU/GPU), network I/O, and storage across major cloud providers. The analysis will employ Patterson's experience in architecture benchmarking and cost-performance analysis, comparing traditional scaling against data-local or near-data processing architectures inspired by earlier RISC and RAID principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, data-driven framework for evaluating the true cost drivers in modern data analysis. It will yield concrete design principles for cost-optimized system architecture, influencing hardware design (e.g., smarter memory hierarchies, network interfaces) and cloud service procurement. The findings will guide funding and research priorities away from pure FLOPS optimization towards holistic data-movement minimization.
proposals.novelty: While individual costs are known, systematically challenging the 'computation is primary' axiom by quantifying the tipping point where data movement costs surpass compute costs across real pipelines is novel. It merges Patterson's architectural expertise with a pragmatic, total-cost business perspective rarely applied to scientific computing.

proposals.title: Kira-Cloud: An Intent-Based Compiler for Performance- and Policy-Aware Multi-Cloud Deployment
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of unique, high-value cloud services (e.g., AWS Lambda, Google TPUs, Azure Cognitive Services) creates a dilemma: developers must choose between vendor lock-in for performance or lowest-common-denominator portability. Pre-2026, tools like Terraform manage infrastructure but require manual, provider-specific blueprints and lack a principled, automated method to optimize for cost and performance across clouds while enforcing portability constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a declarative, intent-based specification, compiled via a continuously updated knowledge graph of cloud capabilities, can automatically generate optimized, provider-specific deployments that inherently preserve operational portability through embedded instrumentation and fallback pathways.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira big-data processing framework to model cloud services as a dynamic, attributed graph, where nodes are services and edges represent functional equivalence, performance relationships, and data transfer costs. A compiler front-end will accept a declarative spec (compute, storage, latency needs) and portability policies. The core will use constraint-solving and graph traversal algorithms (inspired by hardware design-space exploration) to select optimal service combinations across providers. The backend will generate Terraform/Ansible code, embedding instrumentation for monitoring and automated blueprints for failover to a secondary provider configuration.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1) a formal model for cloud capability equivalence and portability, 2) an open-source compiler prototype demonstrating automated, policy-compliant multi-cloud deployment, and 3) a quantitative analysis of the performance/cost trade-offs versus manual or single-cloud deployments using real-world workloads like astronomy image processing.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing Infrastructure-as-Code tools, this approach proactively uses differentiated services while systematically engineering portability in, moving from infrastructure specification to intent-based compilation. It applies Patterson's expertise in architecture exploration and performance (e.g., RISC) to the cloud software stack.

proposals.title: KiraBench: A Generative and Adaptive Benchmarking Framework for Cloud Storage Cost-Performance
proposals.motivation: Cloud storage cost-performance trade-offs are notoriously difficult to predict due to complex, stateful application workloads and the dynamic nature of provider pricing and performance tiers. Existing benchmarks, like YCSB, are static and fail to model the evolving access patterns of modern data pipelines, data lakes, and CI/CD systems, leading to costly misconfigurations. A benchmarking system that can generate realistic, adaptive workloads is critical for both architectural decision-making and for holding providers accountable for their cost-performance claims.
proposals.hypothesis: A generative benchmarking system that models application archetypes as stateful, evolving workload graphs and uses a feedback-driven adaptation engine can produce more predictive and actionable cost-performance analyses than static benchmark suites.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira data processing framework to generate synthetic, stateful I/O traces that mimic the graph-like dependencies of target archetypes (e.g., astronomy pipelines, microservices). An adaptation engine, inspired by cache performance modeling and hardware simulation techniques, will use prior benchmark results to refine workload parameters and a cost model updated via cloud provider APIs. The core architecture will be a feedback loop where the generator, a cost-performance simulator, and a result analyzer iteratively converge on a realistic benchmark profile.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source framework for creating bespoke, predictive storage benchmarks, enabling practitioners to perform accurate what-if analyses for their specific workloads. It will also provide a methodology for continuous benchmarking in dynamic cloud environments, advancing the field beyond point-in-time evaluations.
proposals.novelty: The integration of generative workload modeling based on application graphs with a closed-loop adaptation engine that responds to both historical performance data and real-time cloud pricing changes represents a significant departure from static, one-size-fits-all benchmarking tools.

proposals.title: KiraBench: A Cost-Performance Benchmark Suite for Cloud Storage in Scientific and Commercial Workloads
proposals.motivation: Cloud storage services (e.g., AWS S3, Azure Blob, Google Cloud Storage) offer diverse performance tiers and pricing models, but existing benchmarks like YCSB and fio fail to capture the nuanced cost-performance trade-offs for modern data-intensive applications. This gap makes it difficult for researchers and practitioners, especially in fields like astronomy (e.g., Kira pipeline) and e-commerce, to make informed, cost-effective architectural decisions. A benchmark grounded in real application patterns is urgently needed to guide system design and procurement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a benchmark suite derived from the access patterns of real-world applications (astronomical imagery processing and commercial transaction logs) will reveal non-linear cost-performance Pareto frontiers in cloud storage services, exposing significant optimization opportunities missed by generic benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will instrument the Kira astronomy image processing pipeline and a representative e-commerce logging service to capture detailed I/O traces (request size, sequentiality, read/write ratio, metadata operations). These traces will drive a modular benchmark generator, which will replay patterns at scale while varying parameters like concurrency and data locality. We will integrate real-time cost tracking using cloud provider APIs, enabling the creation of a multi-dimensional cost-performance model. The evaluation will target major cloud object and block storage services, measuring throughput, latency, and dollar cost per operation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source, application-driven benchmark suite that quantifies the true cost of performance for cloud storage. It will provide a methodology for translating application I/O fingerprints into storage tier recommendations, directly aiding architects in minimizing cloud spend. The results will also inform future storage system and cache design for heterogeneous cloud workloads.
proposals.novelty: Unlike synthetic or database-centric benchmarks, KiraBench is built from the ground up using traces from Patterson's own Kira project and commercial systems, directly linking application semantics to storage economics. Its tight integration of real-time cost metering into the benchmarking loop provides a holistic evaluation metric absent from prior work.

proposals.title: Metis: An Adaptive, Feedback-Driven Compilation Framework for Cloud Data Processing
proposals.motivation: Modern cloud data frameworks like Spark and Hadoop rely on static query planning, which cannot adapt to runtime data characteristics (e.g., skew, intermediate data size) or volatile cluster conditions. This leads to inefficient resource use, poor job completion times, and wasted cost in large-scale, data-intensive applications like the astronomical image processing seen in Kira. A more dynamic, adaptive approach is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a runtime system employing continuous profiling and just-in-time (JIT) compilation, inspired by feedback regulation in metabolic pathways, can dynamically reconfigure execution graphs to significantly improve efficiency over static planning in volatile cloud environments.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a prototype framework, Metis, that integrates with Apache Spark. It will use low-overhead runtime profiling to monitor data flow, partition sizes, and cluster resource state. A feedback controller, modeled on biological regulatory networks, will analyze this telemetry to trigger JIT recompilation of the execution DAG using a rule-based and cost-based optimizer. Specific actions include dynamic operator fusion to avoid shuffles, adaptive task splitting for skew mitigation, and cache policy adjustments, all performed during job execution.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel architecture for adaptive cloud data processing, 2) a concrete implementation demonstrating performance gains on real workloads like genomic analysis and astronomical imagery, and 3) a formal analogy between computational resource optimization and biological regulatory systems, providing a new design metaphor for systems software.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored query optimization and straggler mitigation, applying a continuous, feedback-driven JIT compilation model—directly inspired by metabolic pathway regulation—to reconfigure dataflow graphs at runtime is a novel bio-inspired systems approach not seen in pre-2026 big data frameworks.

proposals.title: Kira-T: Temporal Fusion of Asynchronous Lifelog Streams in a Cloud-Native Data-Intensive Architecture
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of wearable sensors and mobile devices generates massive, heterogeneous lifelog streams (video, audio, GPS, biometrics), but deriving contextual insights is bottlenecked by costly pre-processing to align these asynchronous data. Existing big data frameworks like MapReduce and Spark are optimized for key-value processing, not for the complex temporal joins and windowed correlations required for multi-modal analysis at petabyte scale. This limits the real-time, holistic understanding of human behavior and context from personal data.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding native temporal alignment operators directly into the execution engine of a data-intensive computing framework, we can eliminate the alignment pre-processing bottleneck and enable complex, time-sensitive analytics across asynchronous lifelog streams to be expressed and executed efficiently as a single distributed job.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira astronomy image processing framework, which already handles large-scale, irregular data on cloud infrastructure, with new 'TemporalFusion' primitives. These primitives, inspired by temporal join algorithms from multimedia and sensor networks, will operate on data partitions within the distributed cache, aligning streams based on timestamps and uncertainty windows. We will design a new chip/architecture-aware scheduler that co-locates temporally correlated data blocks, minimizing shuffle overhead, and evaluate the system using real-world lifelog datasets (e.g., from the Narrative Clip or Empatica E4) on commercial cloud platforms.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a new, open-source cloud-optimized system (Kira-T) for temporally-aware lifelog analytics, 2) a performance model and benchmark demonstrating order-of-magnitude reductions in alignment latency compared to staged pipelines, and 3) a case study showing scalable derivation of complex contextual patterns from multi-modal data.
proposals.novelty: The fusion moves temporal alignment from an external, sequential pre-processing step into the core of a parallel, data-intensive execution engine, a synthesis not explored in prior big data or lifelogging research. It specifically adapts Patterson's work on domain-specific frameworks (Kira) and hardware-conscious performance to the emerging problem of multi-modal temporal data fusion.

proposals.title: Kira+: A Hardware-Aware, Cache-Optimized Framework for Data-Intensive Astronomy in the Cloud
proposals.motivation: Cloud-based data-intensive frameworks like MapReduce and Spark are foundational for projects like the Kira astronomy pipeline, but their performance often lags behind optimized hardware due to inefficient data movement and obliviousness to underlying architecture. As cloud instances diversify with specialized chips (e.g., GPUs, TPUs) and complex memory hierarchies, a one-size-fits-all software layer wastes resources and limits scientific throughput. This inefficiency contradicts the RISC and industry-academia collaboration principles championed by Patterson, which seek direct, measurable performance gains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting a data-intensive framework (e.g., Spark) with a lightweight, profile-driven scheduler and cache-aware data layout optimizer can significantly reduce job completion time and cost for irregular, graph-like workloads such as astronomical image analysis, by better aligning computation with cloud hardware characteristics.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira/Spark codebase to integrate a runtime profiler that collects hardware performance counters (cache misses, memory bandwidth) and cloud instance metadata. This data will feed a cost model to inform a novel scheduler, co-designing data partitioning and task placement. We will implement a 'cache-conscious' data layout optimizer for intermediate RDDs, inspired by columnar formats and graph partitioning algorithms, to improve locality. The system will be evaluated on Google Cloud using real astronomy datasets from the Kira project, comparing against baseline Spark.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a production-enhanced version of the Kira framework with open-source modifications to Spark, demonstrating measurable performance-per-dollar improvements for a critical scientific workload. It will provide a blueprint for hardware-aware scheduling in cloud data frameworks, bridging the gap between abstract programming models and the evolving physical infrastructure of data centers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes for network or disk I/O, our approach directly targets the memory hierarchy and cache utilization within cloud VMs, a factor often abstracted away. The tight integration of low-level hardware profiling with high-level dataflow graph scheduling for irregular workloads is a distinct, non-obvious co-design.

proposals.title: BFT-Bench: A Benchmarking Framework for Quantifying the Resilience-Performance-Cost Trade-offs of Byzantine Fault Tolerant Cloud Storage
proposals.motivation: Cloud storage systems increasingly underpin critical applications, yet their resilience to Byzantine (arbitrary) faults remains a costly and opaque design choice. While consensus protocols like PBFT and HotStuff offer theoretical guarantees, there is a critical lack of empirical, standardized data on how these protocols impact real-world latency, throughput, and dollar cost in cloud deployments. This gap forces architects to make security decisions without concrete performance and economic data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance and cost overhead of Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT) in cloud storage is not uniform, but varies predictably and significantly based on the specific consensus protocol, storage backend architecture, and the intensity and type of fault injection.
proposals.method: We will design BFT-Bench, a framework extending the principles of Patterson's earlier benchmarking work (e.g., for architecture and cloud) to distributed resilience. The core is a configurable fault injector that emulates Byzantine node behaviors (e.g., equivocation, state corruption) within containerized storage nodes. We will implement and integrate multiple consensus protocols (PBFT, HotStuff, Federated Byzantine Agreement) and measure their impact on standard YCSB workloads, tracking latency, throughput, and cloud infrastructure cost (e.g., AWS/GCP compute/storage pricing). The methodology will systematically vary parameters like cluster size, geographic distribution, and fault rate.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open-source, standardized benchmark for empirically evaluating BFT cloud storage, generating a public dataset of resilience-performance-cost trade-offs. It will provide cloud practitioners with actionable data to select optimal fault-tolerance strategies and offer researchers a reproducible methodology for evaluating new protocols.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that benchmarks performance or fault-tolerance in isolation, BFT-Bench fuses these domains with explicit cost modeling, creating a holistic evaluation framework. It moves beyond crash-fault benchmarks to systematically quantify the tangible overheads of Byzantine resilience, a critical step for its practical adoption.

proposals.title: Kira-Lineage: Applying Scientific Data Provenance to Debug and Audit Cloud-Scale Astronomy Pipelines
proposals.motivation: Modern cloud data pipelines, like those in the Kira astronomy project, are complex, distributed, and prone to silent errors in data transformation and movement. Debugging these pipelines or auditing results for reproducibility is notoriously difficult without a systematic record of data provenance. Techniques from scientific workflows (e.g., from bioinformatics or physics) have long addressed similar challenges in high-performance computing environments but are not optimized for the scale and ephemeral nature of cloud execution.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting and extending fine-grained data lineage capture methods from scientific workflow systems, we can create a low-overhead framework that enables precise debugging and causal auditing of errors in cloud-scale data pipelines, significantly reducing mean-time-to-diagnosis for pipeline faults.
proposals.method: We will design Kira-Lineage, a library that instruments pipeline stages (e.g., Apache Spark operations in Kira) to emit lineage metadata, capturing data dependencies and transformations. This builds on Patterson's experience with performance analysis and big data systems. We will implement a hybrid logging architecture, using compact in-memory structures for runtime capture (leveraging hardware cache efficiency insights) and a persistent graph database backend for querying lineage. The core algorithm will adapt prior graph-based provenance models to handle the dynamic, partitioned dataflows common in cloud pipelines.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, open-source tool for data lineage in cloud pipelines, validated on real astronomy workloads from Kira. It will contribute a methodology for retrofitting provenance into existing big data frameworks and a quantitative analysis of the performance overhead versus debugging utility trade-off in production environments.
proposals.novelty: While data provenance is a known concept, its application is novel in the context of Patterson's Kira project and commercial-scale cloud data pipelines, focusing on a hardware-conscious, low-overhead implementation designed for immediate practical use rather than as a standalone research system.

proposals.title: Kira-Fabric: An Intent-Based, Opportunistic Scheduler for Personal-Edge Graph Processing
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of personal and IoT devices creates a vast, underutilized compute fabric at the edge, but existing cloud-centric models incur latency, cost, and privacy penalties for personal data analytics. Current edge computing frameworks require static infrastructure or heavy, persistent orchestration stacks, failing to harness the dynamic, heterogeneous collection of devices a user owns or trusts. This is critical for latency-sensitive applications like real-time astronomy image processing (ala Kira) or genomic analysis on personal devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lightweight, intent-based scheduler using a declarative constraint API can efficiently and securely compose ephemeral compute fabrics from opportunistic personal-edge resources, matching the performance of fixed micro-clouds for data-local tasks while preserving user privacy and control.
proposals.method: We will design a decentralized scheduler architecture inspired by Patterson's RISC and warehouse-scale computer principles, but for dynamic, personal-scale systems. The core is a constraint solver that maps application intents (e.g., 'process this image graph within 100ms, data must not leave my home network') onto a dynamically discovered resource graph. We will implement a gossip-based discovery protocol and a lightweight, secure function runtime (similar to unikernels or WebAssembly) for task placement. Performance evaluation will use modified Kira astronomy and genomic pipeline workloads on a testbed of Raspberry Pis, laptops, and phones.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel scheduling architecture and protocol for intent-based, opportunistic personal-edge computing. 2) Quantitative performance and privacy trade-off analysis compared to static edge and cloud deployments. 3) An open-source reference implementation and declarative API specification, extending the 'bringing industry back' model by providing a framework for commercial personal-edge products.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static edge clusters or serverless clouds, this system treats a user's transient collection of devices as a single, schedulable resource fabric without requiring a persistent Kubernetes-like stack, focusing on declarative intent rather than imperative orchestration for personal data processing.

proposals.title: CapNet: A Bio-Inspired, Hierarchical Architecture for Autonomous Resource Arbitration in Edge Micro-Clouds
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of edge devices and latency-sensitive applications (e.g., AR/VR, IoT analytics) demands a shift from centralized clouds to decentralized, private micro-clouds. However, scaling cloud architecture down to the edge faces critical challenges in low-latency resource arbitration, fault tolerance, and data privacy without a central orchestrator. Existing edge frameworks lack the efficient, autonomous, and fine-grained resource-sharing mechanisms needed for this highly distributed environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computing architecture modeled after the human capillary network's hierarchical, demand-regulated resource distribution will enable a resilient, low-latency fabric of edge micro-clouds capable of autonomous resource arbitration, outperforming both centralized cloud offload and static peer-to-peer edge clusters in performance and privacy for localized workloads.
proposals.method: We will design and simulate the CapNet architecture, defining 'capillary' (single device), 'arteriole' (local cluster), and 'venous' (wider-area) hierarchical layers. A dual-priority task routing protocol, inspired by oxygenated/deoxygenated blood flow, will prioritize local processing and neighbor-assisted compute recycling. We will implement software-defined 'sphincter' policies at node boundaries to control data flow based on latency, privacy, and load. The system will be evaluated using an adapted version of the Kira astronomy image processing pipeline to model bursty, data-intensive edge workloads, measuring latency, throughput, and resilience against node failure.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architectural blueprint and arbitration protocol for capillary-scale distributed computing. It will provide a concrete framework for building privacy-preserving, low-latency edge fabrics and deliver quantitative evidence on the performance trade-offs of bio-inspired, decentralized control versus centralized orchestration in edge environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct physiological analogy applied to compute resource arbitration, moving beyond simple peer-to-peer or federated learning models to a hierarchical, flow-regulated system with explicit local policy 'sphincters.' This approach is distinct from prior edge computing research focused on scheduling or offloading to a central cloud or static fog layer.

proposals.title: Kira-Micro: A Scalable, Low-Latency Micro-Cloud Architecture for Personal and Edge Astronomy Data Processing
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of high-resolution, data-intensive sensors in astronomy (e.g., LSST, SKA pathfinders) and personal devices creates a bottleneck for centralized cloud processing, introducing latency, bandwidth costs, and privacy concerns for raw data. Existing edge computing solutions lack the principled, scalable architecture and fault-tolerant programming models pioneered in Patterson's prior cloud and data-center work. There is a critical need to bring cloud-like manageability and performance to the extreme edge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a micro-cloud architecture, derived from scalable data-center principles but radically miniaturized for resource-constrained edge nodes, can provide low-latency, private data processing for astronomy imagery and similar workloads while maintaining the programmability and reliability of large-scale cloud systems.
proposals.method: We will design a reference micro-cloud hardware/software stack, leveraging RISC-V-based chiplet architectures for modular, energy-efficient compute. The software core will be a scaled-down, hardened version of the Kira astronomy processing pipeline, optimized for in-memory operation on a single node with aggressive cache-conscious algorithms. We will implement a lightweight, fault-tolerant orchestration layer inspired by industry cluster managers, enabling dynamic task scheduling across a personal cluster of micro-cloud devices (e.g., a home astronomy setup). Performance and scalability will be evaluated against traditional cloud-offload models using latency, throughput, and energy efficiency metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel systems architecture blueprint for personal/edge micro-clouds, 2) a production-quality, open-source implementation of the Kira pipeline for edge deployment, and 3) a quantitative analysis of the trade-offs between micro-cloud and centralized cloud processing for latency-sensitive, data-private applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying Patterson's deep expertise in datacenter-scale architecture (RISC, RAID, clusters) and big-data processing (Kira) to the opposite extreme—the personal edge—creating a unified architectural philosophy from data center to device, which is absent in contemporary fragmented edge computing research.

proposals.title: Kira-Cost: A Dual Optimization Framework for Astronomy Imaging Pipelines on Heterogeneous Cloud Hardware
proposals.motivation: Cloud providers now offer a diverse array of specialized hardware (e.g., GPUs, FPGAs, custom AI chips), but selecting the optimal, cost-minimizing instance to meet a strict performance target remains a complex, manual challenge. This is critical for data-intensive scientific workloads like the Kira astronomy pipeline, where processing must meet throughput deadlines for sky surveys under constrained budgets. Current auto-scaling and instance selection tools are largely architecture-agnostic, leading to significant over-provisioning and cost inefficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by constructing fine-grained performance models for specific workload kernels (e.g., image convolution, source extraction) across heterogeneous hardware and integrating them with a cost-aware scheduler, we can automatically generate hardware assignments that meet fixed performance targets at significantly lower financial cost than homogeneous or architecture-blind selection.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira astronomy image processing framework to instrument key computational kernels. Using controlled profiling on AWS EC2 instances (general-purpose, GPU, FPGA, Graviton), we will build a performance prediction model based on workload characteristics (compute intensity, data locality). This model will feed into a dual-optimization scheduler, formulated as a constrained minimization problem, that selects the hardware architecture and instance count to minimize dollar cost subject to a user-defined latency/throughput target, using techniques like integer linear programming or heuristic search.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source optimization framework for cost-performance trade-offs on heterogeneous cloud hardware, validated on a real-world astronomy pipeline. It will provide a methodology for architectural selection beyond mere sizing, contributing new benchmarks and cost models for specialized instances in scientific computing.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior cost optimization work focused on homogeneous fleets, this research explicitly solves the architectural selection problem, fusing performance modeling of diverse hardware (CPU, GPU, FPGA) with cloud pricing models. It moves beyond 'paying for results' to actively minimizing the cost of achieving them.

proposals.title: Kira-Edge: Millisecond Serverless for Astronomy Image Processing via Pre-Provisioned Edge Contexts
proposals.motivation: Serverless computing is hindered by network latency and cold-start overheads, making it unsuitable for interactive, data-intensive tasks like real-time astronomy image processing (e.g., Kira pipelines). The proliferation of edge hardware and specialized accelerators (e.g., TPUs, FPGAs) presents an opportunity to bring computation closer to data sources, but current serverless models lack the deterministic, millisecond-scale execution required for responsive scientific workflows.
proposals.hypothesis: By pre-deploying secure, minimal runtime contexts (e.g., Python, WebAssembly) on edge nodes and using a lightweight local dispatcher for task-to-context matching via shared memory, we can achieve true millisecond-scale serverless task execution while maintaining the programming semantics and elasticity of the cloud backend for longer-running tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a two-tier architecture consisting of a 'Proximal Runtime Pool' on edge servers and a traditional cloud backend. The edge pool will host pre-warmed, isolated contexts using hardware-enforced security (e.g., Intel SGX, AMD SEV). A local dispatcher, inspired by cache coherence protocols, will use a fast classification algorithm (based on task code hash and resource requirements) to match incoming micro-tasks to a compatible warm context for sub-millisecond IPC execution. For tasks exceeding a local resource threshold, the system will seamlessly route them to the cloud, employing a performance model akin to cache miss handling.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel serverless architecture that demonstrably achieves sub-10-millisecond task execution latencies, a quantitative performance model for proximal vs. cloud execution trade-offs, and an open-source prototype evaluated using real-world astronomy image processing pipelines from the Kira project.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior edge computing or serverless work, this system integrates pre-provisioned, secure edge contexts directly into the serverless abstraction, enabling transparent, semantics-preserving execution with deterministic low latency, a approach not seen in pre-2026 FaaS platforms like AWS Lambda or OpenWhisk.

proposals.title: Kira-F: A Fine-Grained Serverless Architecture for Millisecond-Scale Astronomy Image Processing
proposals.motivation: Current serverless platforms (e.g., AWS Lambda) incur significant cold-start and orchestration overheads, making them inefficient for fine-grained, data-intensive tasks like processing individual CCD readouts or transient events in astronomy pipelines like Kira. The rise of specialized, data-centric architectures (e.g., CXL, Smart NICs, and in-memory processing) creates an opportunity to re-architect the serverless model from the hardware up for extreme scale-down.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing a serverless runtime with a lightweight, cache-coherent hardware substrate, we can execute millisecond-scale data-processing tasks with near-bare-metal efficiency, making fine-grained serverless economically viable for high-throughput scientific and commercial workloads.
proposals.method: We will design and simulate a 'Scale-Down Serverless' architecture extending Patterson's RISC-V and hardware specialization themes. The method involves: 1) A new 'NanoLambda' runtime that bypasses OS and container overhead for pre-validated functions, 2) A hardware-assisted dispatch mechanism using a shared, cache-coherent address space (inspired by CXL 3.0) for sub-millisecond data access between functions, and 3) A scheduling algorithm that groups fine-grained tasks from a workflow (e.g., a Kira image reduction pipeline) to minimize data movement, leveraging hardware performance counters for real-time optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel serverless architecture blueprint that pushes the granularity boundary from seconds to milliseconds. It will provide a quantitative framework for evaluating the hardware-software co-design of serverless systems and deliver an open-source simulation model for fine-grained, data-intensive workloads like astronomical image processing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating millisecond tasks as a first-class architectural design point for serverless, moving beyond software-only optimization to a hardware-software co-design that exploits emerging cache-coherent interconnects and lightweight RISC-V cores, an approach not yet explored in pre-2026 FaaS research.

proposals.title: Kira-P: A Federated, User-Centric Cloud Architecture for Scalable and Private Lifelog Processing
proposals.motivation: Lifelogging generates massive, multimodal data streams (video, audio, biometrics) that overwhelm personal devices and create privacy risks when centralized. Current solutions are siloed, application-specific, and lack the computational scale for longitudinal AI analysis. A new infrastructure paradigm is needed to enable large-scale, private processing of a user's complete digital footprint.
proposals.hypothesis: A vertically scaled, user-controlled federation of commercial cloud resources can provide the computational and storage scale for holistic lifelog analysis while preserving privacy through data federation and in-situ processing, moving beyond the limitations of single-provider or device-centric models.
proposals.method: We will design a 'personal mega-cloud' architecture inspired by federated systems and the data-partitioning strategies of projects like Kira. The core is a secure orchestrator that dynamically allocates data streams and tasks (e.g., video encoding, audio transcription, model training) across a user's allocated resources from multiple cloud providers (e.g., AWS, Google Cloud, a private server). We will develop a cache-coherent data management layer and scheduling algorithms to optimize for locality, cost, and latency, treating the federation as a single, large-scale system for the individual user.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel system architecture blueprint for user-centric data clouds, a set of scheduling and data placement policies for federated personal resources, and an evaluation framework quantifying the trade-offs between performance, privacy, and cost for large-scale lifelogging. It shifts the focus from application logs to a unified personal data infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While federated learning distributes model training, we federate the underlying storage and compute infrastructure itself for a single user, applying data-center-scale system design principles to the personal domain. This merges the hardware/cloud expertise of Patterson's work with the big-data processing ethos of Kira for a new, privacy-first use case.

proposals.title: Kira-Fabric: A Personal Compute Fabric Middleware for Dynamic Orchestration of Heterogeneous Edge Devices
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of personal specialized hardware (GPUs, NPUs, FPGAs) creates underutilized, fragmented compute resources. Current cloud-centric orchestration systems are ill-suited for the dynamic, ad-hoc, and latency-sensitive environment of personal devices. This research addresses the need for a system that can unify a user's personal hardware into a coherent, self-optimizing compute platform, enabling new applications in personal AI, real-time media processing, and citizen science, as explored in projects like Kira for astronomy.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight, broker-based middleware, inspired by enterprise integration patterns and informed by hardware performance modeling, can effectively abstract heterogeneous personal devices into a single 'compute fabric,' enabling automatic, context-aware task placement that significantly improves performance-per-watt and reduces latency for personal workloads.
proposals.method: We will design a publish-subscribe middleware with a smart broker that maintains a dynamic registry of device capabilities (inspired by Patterson's work on hardware characterization). The broker will use a multi-objective scheduling algorithm, considering real-time power, latency, and computational affinity, to match tasks to devices. We will prototype the system using Raspberry Pi clusters with attached accelerators (e.g., Intel NCS, Coral TPU) and evaluate it using workloads derived from the Kira astronomy image processing pipeline and real-time AI inference tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel architecture for personal heterogeneous computing, 2) a practical, open-source middleware system for dynamic edge orchestration, and 3) a performance model and evaluation of scheduling policies for ad-hoc personal compute fabrics, bridging the gap between data-center cloud methods and personal device constraints.
proposals.novelty: While cloud orchestration is mature, applying these principles to a dynamic, personally-owned set of devices with extreme heterogeneity and voluntary participation is novel. The system's design is inspired by enterprise service buses for software, but applied to the novel domain of hardware resource integration for the individual user.

proposals.title: Kira-G: Achieving Domain-Specific Efficiency on General-Purpose Clusters via Graph-Optimized Runtime Specialization
proposals.motivation: The dominance of specialized hardware (e.g., TPUs, FPGAs) for performance-critical tasks like astronomy image processing (Kira) creates high costs, vendor lock-in, and accessibility barriers. However, the massive scale and data-parallel nature of these problems share similarities with graph and big data workloads already efficiently processed on general-purpose cloud clusters. We must determine if novel software abstractions can close the efficiency gap, reducing reliance on hardware specialization.
proposals.hypothesis: That a runtime system leveraging fine-grained, dynamic dependency graphs and cache-aware scheduling can orchestrate general-purpose CPU/GPU clusters to match the performance-per-watt of fixed-function accelerators for specific, irregular data-parallel workloads like astronomical image analysis.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira architecture to model all computation and data movement as a fine-grained dynamic dataflow graph. A novel runtime scheduler, informed by real-time cache locality models and memory bandwidth telemetry, will optimize task placement and prefetching across a heterogeneous cluster. We will implement this using a modified version of Apache Spark's RDD abstraction for fault tolerance, but with a much more aggressive, hardware-conscious executor inspired by cache-efficient graph processing frameworks like GraphLab.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete software architecture and open-source prototype demonstrating how to achieve near-specialized hardware efficiency on commodity clouds for a critical scientific domain. It will contribute new scheduling algorithms for cache- and bandwidth-optimized execution of irregular pipelines, broadening access to high-performance computing.
proposals.novelty: While prior work specializes hardware or uses static compilers, our approach uniquely combines dynamic, dataflow-based runtime scheduling with real-time hardware telemetry on unmodified clusters, treating the entire data center as a malleable, software-defined accelerator.

proposals.title: Kira-Chip: A Single-Package Warehouse-Scale Computer for Edge Astronomy Processing
proposals.motivation: The analysis of massive astronomy datasets, as in the Kira project, is bottlenecked by data movement to and from centralized cloud datacenters, incurring high latency and bandwidth costs. Emerging edge computing and specialized hardware trends, like Google's TPU pods and advanced packaging (e.g., HBM, chiplets), suggest a potential paradigm shift. A compact, integrated system could enable real-time processing at remote observatories, transforming data-intensive scientific workflows.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single-package system integrating heterogeneous compute cores, a multi-level cache-coherent memory hierarchy, and a high-bandwidth network-on-chip can deliver the programmability and performance isolation of a warehouse-scale computer while achieving orders-of-magnitude improvements in performance-per-watt and latency for targeted workloads like astronomical image analysis.
proposals.method: We will design a chiplet-based architecture using advanced 2.5D/3D packaging. The design will incorporate: 1) Heterogeneous chiplets (general-purpose RISC-V cores, GPU-style accelerators, and custom units for graph processing and image convolution), 2) A unified, cache-coherent address space across all chiplets using a scalable directory protocol, and 3) A Kilo-NOC style network-on-package for low-latency, high-bandwidth communication. We will develop a simulation framework based on gem5 and FireSim to model the full hierarchy and evaluate performance on Kira's astronomy pipeline.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel architectural blueprint for a 'micro-datacenter' and an open-source simulation framework for evaluating tightly integrated heterogeneous systems. It will provide concrete performance and efficiency projections for edge scientific computing, demonstrating a viable path beyond von Neumann and monolithic server architectures for specific, data-intensive domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores scale-up (TPU Pods) and scale-out (cloud), this proposal uniquely focuses on radical scale-down: condensing the key architectural principles of a WSC—heterogeneity, hierarchy, and software-defined infrastructure—into a single package for domain-specific edge deployment, moving beyond accelerators to integrated systems.

proposals.title: Kira-Edge: A Performance and Cost Analysis of Astronomy Image Processing on a Geographically Distributed Micro-Cloud
proposals.motivation: The dominant cloud model relies on massive, centralized data centers, creating latency for edge data sources like astronomical observatories and incurring significant data transfer costs. Pre-2026 trends show increasing compute capability in small form factors (e.g., ARM-based servers, NVIDIA Jetson) and growing interest in edge computing, but no systematic study exists for constructing a 'cloud' from thousands of such nodes distributed at potential observation sites.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for data-intensive, embarrassingly parallel workloads like astronomy image processing (e.g., the Kira pipeline), a cloud composed of globally distributed, micro-scale compute nodes can achieve lower total cost of analysis (including data movement) and comparable aggregate performance to a traditional centralized cloud, while improving data locality and access latency.
proposals.method: We will design a simulation framework modeling a global network of micro-nodes (e.g., Raspberry Pi clusters or low-power ARM servers) co-located with telescope facilities. Using real-world astronomy data ingestion patterns and the existing Kira pipeline as a benchmark, we will develop a scheduling algorithm that optimizes for data locality, network transfer costs, and node heterogeneity. We will compare this against simulated executions on traditional cloud architectures (AWS, Google Cloud) using pre-2026 pricing models and performance data for cache hierarchies and interconnects relevant to both centralized and distributed setups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative performance and cost model for a radically decentralized cloud architecture applied to a real scientific big-data problem. It will deliver a novel scheduling algorithm for geo-distributed micro-compute and a concrete analysis framework to guide future infrastructure investments for data-intensive sciences.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the core cloud assumption—moving computation to a few giant data centers—to moving micro-data centers to the computation. This contrasts with prior edge computing work focused on IoT or latency-sensitive apps, applying it instead to large-scale batch scientific processing typically reserved for centralized hyperscale clouds.

proposals.title: The Cloud Performance Decathlon: A Benchmark Suite for Measuring and Comparing Heterogeneous Cloud Services
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, cloud computing is the default platform for scientific and commercial workloads, yet performance evaluation remains fragmented and vendor-specific. Researchers and practitioners lack standardized, holistic benchmarks to make informed cost-performance trade-offs across providers, especially for emerging data-intensive workloads like graph analytics and genomics, which Patterson's prior work (e.g., Kira) has highlighted. This impedes scientific reproducibility and efficient resource procurement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed, multi-faceted benchmark suite, co-designed with hardware and workload characteristics in mind, can reveal significant, previously obscured performance disparities and cost inefficiencies across major cloud service providers.
proposals.method: We will design a benchmark suite comprising ten distinct workloads, including graph processing (using Graph500-like algorithms), genomic sequence alignment (BWA-MEM), astronomy image processing (extending Kira's pipeline), and key-value store operations. Each benchmark will be instrumented to measure not just execution time, but also hardware counter data (via perf), cache hierarchy efficiency, and I/O patterns. We will deploy the suite on identical VM configurations across AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure, using orchestration tools like Kubernetes for consistency, and analyze the architectural implications of observed performance differences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the research community and industry with a rigorous, open-source benchmark suite for cross-cloud comparison. It will contribute a detailed architectural analysis of performance variability, linking it to underlying hardware (chip, cache, process) differences. The results will establish a foundation for more predictable cloud computing and guide future co-design of applications and cloud infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing micro-benchmarks or single-provider tools, this proposal creates a holistic, application-driven "decathlon" specifically designed to stress heterogeneous cloud architectures. Its novelty lies in the co-design of benchmarks from Patterson's diverse research themes (graph, genomic, astronomy) to uncover systemic hardware-software interactions across commercial clouds.

proposals.title: Kira-Cloud: A Declarative, Hardware-Aware Programming Model for Scalable Graph and Image Processing in the Cloud
proposals.motivation: Cloud infrastructure offers immense scale but forces developers into a complex trade-off between low-level resource management (VMs, caches, processes) and opaque, high-level serverless abstractions. This complexity is acute for irregular, data-intensive workloads like astronomy image analysis (as in the Kira project) and large-scale graph processing, hindering performance portability and efficient resource utilization. A new programming model is needed to bridge this gap, simplifying development while exposing hardware-aware optimizations.
proposals.hypothesis: A declarative, data-centric programming model that abstracts compute resources into logical dataflow graphs, coupled with a runtime that performs automated, cost-aware mapping onto heterogeneous cloud hardware (CPUs, GPUs, specialized chips), can significantly simplify application development while achieving performance and efficiency comparable to hand-tuned implementations.
proposals.method: We will design a domain-specific language (DSL) extending the Kira/Spark paradigm, where users specify computations and data dependencies declaratively. A novel compiler will analyze this specification to construct an intermediate representation modeled as a dynamic dataflow graph. The runtime system, inspired by RISC principles of hardware-software co-design, will profile underlying instance types (CPU architectures, cache hierarchies, GPU availability) and use a cost-based optimizer to partition and schedule graph fragments. We will implement a prototype using Apache Spark and evaluate it on astronomy image pipelines and genomic variant-calling graphs, comparing developer effort and performance against manual AWS Lambda/EC2 setups and vanilla Spark.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel programming model and runtime architecture for hardware-aware cloud computing, 2) a set of concrete algorithms for cost-optimal dataflow scheduling on heterogeneous cloud resources, and 3) an open-source framework and case studies demonstrating simplified development for graph and image processing workloads, providing a blueprint for the next generation of cloud-native applications.
