proposals.novelty: Unlike existing serverless or monolithic dataflow frameworks, this approach tightly integrates a declarative user interface with a runtime that makes explicit, dynamic optimization decisions based on a detailed model of underlying and variable cloud hardware, moving beyond simple VM scheduling to true application-to-silicon co-design in the cloud.

proposals.title: Kira-Edge: A Cost and Latency-Aware Framework for Inverse Offloading of Astronomy Image Processing from Cloud to Client Devices
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in big data analytics, including projects like Kira for astronomy, is to move data and computation to the cloud. However, this incurs significant and recurring data transfer costs, creates latency for interactive analysis, and underutilizes the growing computational power (e.g., multi-core CPUs, GPUs) and storage (NVMe SSDs) available on researcher workstations and edge clusters. A systematic, performance-driven strategy for the inverse problem—offloading suitable computations from the cloud back to the edge—is needed to reduce costs and improve responsiveness for data-intensive science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing a fine-grained cost model incorporating data transfer latency, cloud compute pricing, and client hardware capabilities, we can identify and efficiently offload specific sub-graphs of astronomy image processing pipelines (e.g., localized source extraction, image filtering) from cloud VMs back to authenticated client devices, achieving significant cost savings and lower latency without compromising result accuracy.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira architecture with an 'Inverse Scheduler' that profiles the performance and cost of pipeline stages. Using a graph partitioning algorithm (e.g., METIS) on the computational DAG, weighted by our cost model, it will decide which subgraphs to execute on pre-registered, trusted client devices. We will implement a secure, containerized (Docker) task runtime for clients and modify Kira's execution engine to manage hybrid cloud-edge workflows, using cache-aware data chunking to minimize transfer overhead for the offloaded computations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a practical framework and cost model for inverse offloading in scientific big data processing, 2) an extension of the Kira system demonstrating hybrid cloud-client execution, and 3) a quantitative evaluation of cost/performance trade-offs using real astronomy workloads (e.g., from the Palomar Transient Factory) on cloud platforms (AWS, Google Cloud) and modern client hardware.
proposals.novelty: While edge-to-cloud offloading is well-studied, the inverse problem for cost-reduction in big-data science is not. This proposal uniquely combines Patterson's expertise in architecture, performance, and the Kira system with a concrete, scheduler-driven approach to leverage underutilized client-side resources, flipping the standard cloud-centric model.

proposals.title: Kira-Edge: A Partition-Aware Architecture for Intermittently Connected Astronomical Data Processing
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of remote observatories and mobile sensor platforms in astronomy and earth sciences creates a data processing challenge. These environments often suffer from high-latency, low-bandwidth, or completely partitioned network connections to the cloud, rendering continuous streaming and centralized big data frameworks like Spark ineffective. This work addresses the need for robust, edge-capable architectures that maintain scientific throughput despite unreliable connectivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid mobile-cloud architecture, built on a partition-tolerant dataflow model and intelligent caching of intermediate computational states, can deliver predictable performance and correctness for data-intensive tasks like astronomical image reduction under intermittent connectivity.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira astronomy processing framework with a new 'Edge Kira' agent. This agent uses a novel, hardware-conscious cache architecture (inspired by Patterson's earlier work) to persist resilient distributed datasets (RDD) lineage and checkpointed intermediate results locally on edge devices. A lightweight, asynchronous coordination protocol will manage task reallocation between edge and cloud based on connectivity heuristics. We will prototype the system on a Raspberry Pi cluster simulating a remote observatory, using real astronomy image datasets and controlled network partitions to evaluate performance against a baseline cloud-only Kira deployment.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a formal model for partition-aware scientific dataflow, 2) a reference implementation of the Kira-Edge architecture, and 3) a quantitative performance analysis showing trade-offs between local compute resources, cache size, and tolerance to network downtime. It will provide a blueprint for adapting other cloud-native, big-data scientific workflows to challenging field environments.
proposals.novelty: While edge computing and disconnected operation are known, applying these principles to the specific domain of astronomical data processing—with its unique data dependencies and accuracy requirements—and integrating them directly into a proven big-data framework like Kira is novel. The focus on caching computational lineage, not just raw data, for resumption is a key architectural insight.

proposals.title: Kira-Edge: A Cloud-in-a-Box Architecture Using Scaled Heterogeneous Mobile SoCs for Edge Astronomy Processing
proposals.motivation: The processing of large-scale astronomy imagery, as in the Kira project, often requires cloud-scale resources, but field deployments (e.g., remote observatories) face prohibitive latency, bandwidth, and cost for cloud offload. Meanwhile, mobile System-on-Chips (SoCs) integrate high-efficiency CPUs, GPUs, and specialized accelerators but are designed for single-device power envelopes. A scalable, integrated node built from these components could bring datacenter-class processing to the edge.
proposals.hypothesis: That a dense, rack-scale compute node composed of tightly integrated, scaled-up mobile SoC dies can achieve superior performance-per-watt and performance-per-dollar for specific irregular, data-intensive workloads (like astronomical graph analysis) compared to traditional homogeneous server processors, while enabling a self-contained 'cloud-in-a-box' deployment model.
proposals.method: We will design a prototype board and system architecture that aggregates multiple commercial mobile SoC dies (e.g., Apple M-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon, or Samsung Exynos architectures) via a high-bandwidth, cache-coherent interconnect fabric, scaling a single mobile chip's heterogeneous core complex. We will develop a tailored runtime and scheduler, extending prior work on big data frameworks (e.g., Apache Spark used in Kira), to efficiently map workload stages (like image correlation graph processing) to the appropriate mix of CPU, GPU, and DSP cores across the scaled fabric. Performance and efficiency will be benchmarked against standard Xeon and EPYC servers using real astronomy pipelines.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel system architecture blueprint and open-source software stack for scalable heterogeneous mobile SoC clusters. It will provide quantitative analysis of the trade-offs in performance, energy efficiency, and cost for irregular data analytics at the edge, directly informing the design of future specialized edge data centers.
proposals.novelty: While mobile SoCs are studied in isolation and scale-out clusters use discrete servers, this proposal uniquely investigates scaling *up* the integrated mobile SoC fabric itself to create a single, high-density node, blending datacenter node design with ultra-low-power processor philosophy for a new edge appliance class.

proposals.title: CacheLogP: Extending the LogP Model with Hierarchical Memory and Cache-Awareness for Modern Cloud Workloads
proposals.motivation: The LogP model, foundational for parallel algorithm design, abstracts communication costs but omits critical memory hierarchy effects, treating all memory accesses as uniform. This is a severe limitation for modern data-intensive workloads, such as graph analytics and genomic processing, which are heavily bottlenecked by cache misses and NUMA effects in cloud and server-scale hardware. A more accurate model is needed to guide efficient algorithm and system architecture design for these prevalent applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the LogP model with parameters for cache capacity, latency hierarchy, and data locality will yield significantly more accurate performance predictions for data-centric parallel workloads on modern multi-core and many-core architectures, enabling the design of more efficient algorithms.
proposals.method: We will extend LogP to CacheLogP by adding parameters for cache size (C), hierarchy depth (H), and a locality factor (λ). We will validate the model using microbenchmarks and real workloads, including the Kira astronomy image processing pipeline and GraphX-style analytics, on diverse hardware (from Intel Xeon to emerging cloud instances). The analysis will employ performance counters (e.g., via PAPI) to correlate model predictions with actual cycles lost to memory stalls, and will explore automated tuning of algorithm parameters (like block size) based on the CacheLogP parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, refined parallel cost model that captures memory system behavior, filling a known gap in classical theory. It will deliver a set of guidelines and potentially compiler/runtime extensions for designing cache-optimal parallel algorithms for big data and scientific computing tasks, directly impacting fields like genomics and astronomy that Patterson's group has engaged with.
proposals.novelty: While cache-aware algorithms exist, integrating hierarchical memory costs directly into the canonical LogP model to create a unified design tool for both communication and memory-bound phases is novel. This bridges the gap between abstract parallel theory and the concrete architectural realities of pre-2026 cloud and server hardware.

proposals.title: Kira-P: Automated Performance Prediction and Optimization for Parallel Astronomy Pipelines Using a Refined LogP Model
proposals.motivation: The analysis of massive astronomy datasets, as in the Kira project, requires efficient parallel execution on diverse cloud and cluster hardware. Manually tuning code for each architecture is time-consuming and error-prone. An automated tool that can predict performance and guide optimization is critical for scaling scientific discovery in the pre-exascale era.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a compiler-integrated tool, using an extended LogP model that incorporates cache hierarchy effects and network topology, can accurately predict the performance of parallel data-processing kernels and automatically generate architecture-specific optimizations.
proposals.method: We will extend the classic LogP model with parameters for cache capacity/bandwidth (C) and network topology (T), creating a LogP-CT framework. This model will be integrated into the LLVM compiler infrastructure. We will develop a static analysis pass to extract computational graphs from astronomy pipelines (e.g., image coaddition) and feed them, along with measured machine parameters, into a performance predictor. The tool will then suggest optimizations like loop tiling, communication aggregation, and task granularity adjustments, validated on cloud instances and research clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source, practical tool for performance prediction and optimization of data-intensive parallel codes. It will advance the state of analytical performance modeling by integrating it directly into a production compiler. The results will provide a methodology for bringing hardware-aware auto-tuning to domain-specific scientific workflows.
proposals.novelty: While LogP is well-studied, its integration into a mainstream compiler for automatic optimization of real-world data science pipelines is novel. The extension to explicitly model cache effects and topology, driven by the specific needs of astronomical image processing, moves beyond traditional HPC kernels.

proposals.title: Revisiting LogP for Exascale: Modeling and Algorithmic Adaptations for Million-Processor Systems
proposals.motivation: The push towards exascale computing, with systems like the planned U.S. Exascale Computing Project machines, presents architectures with millions of heterogeneous cores and deep, hierarchical networks. The classic LogP model, while foundational for parallel algorithm design, was conceived for smaller-scale, more homogeneous systems and may not capture the dominant costs—such as network contention, synchronization overheads, and deep cache hierarchies—at this extreme scale. This gap hinders the design of efficient algorithms for key data-intensive workloads, like those in astronomy (e.g., Kira) and genomics, which Patterson has previously explored using big-data frameworks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a refined 'ExaLogP' model, incorporating parameters for network contention, topology-aware latency, and hierarchical synchronization costs, will enable the design of communication-optimal algorithms that maintain performance portability across pre-exascale and emerging exascale architectures.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a detailed performance analysis on pre-exascale systems (e.g., cloud-based clusters and large supercomputers) to quantify the divergence of real communication patterns from classic LogP predictions. Using this data, we will extend the LogP model with new parameters (e.g., 'C' for contention) and validate it via discrete-event simulation. We will then redesign core graph and linear algebra kernels (inspired by Kira's processing patterns) for the new model, implementing and benchmarking them on large-scale emulated platforms using architectural simulation frameworks like gem5 or SST, focusing on cache-efficient and topology-aware communication.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield: 1) A validated, extended LogP model for million-processor systems, providing a crucial tool for architects and programmers; 2) A set of adapted algorithmic templates for fundamental operations, demonstrating significant performance improvements over naive LogP-based designs in simulation; 3) Concrete guidelines for hardware-software co-design of exascale systems, bridging Patterson's expertise in architecture and big-data processing.
proposals.novelty: While LogP extensions exist, none systematically model the combined impact of network contention and deep memory hierarchies at the million-processor scale for data-intensive workloads. This proposal uniquely integrates architectural modeling with practical algorithm redesign for domains like astronomical imaging, directly building on Patterson's recent work in commercial-scale data processing.

proposals.title: Kira-SGX: A Hardware-Accelerated Architecture for Scalable, Secure Cloud Astronomy Processing
proposals.motivation: Cloud-based processing of massive astronomy datasets, as in the Kira project, is increasingly common but faces a critical security-performance trade-off. Sensitive observational data must be processed within trusted execution environments (TEEs) like Intel SGX, but their performance overhead, especially for memory-intensive graph and image processing workloads, is prohibitive at data-center scale. This limits the practical adoption of secure enclaves for large-scale scientific computing in shared cloud infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting the custom co-processor design principles from specialized hardware (like Google's TPU or prior RISC designs) into a scalable, enclave-aware architectural extension for multi-core server CPUs, we can dramatically reduce the trusted computing base (TCB) and performance penalty of securing graph-based astronomy pipelines without sacrificing programmability.
proposals.method: We will design a lightweight, in-package accelerator core, codenamed Kira-SGX, integrated with a cloud server CPU socket. This core will implement a RISC-V ISA extension optimized for the key graph traversal and pixel operation kernels identified in Kira's astronomy workflows. The core will feature a dedicated, encrypted cache hierarchy and memory controller that operates within the CPU's SGX enclave, allowing secure data to never leave the accelerated, protected hardware domain. We will evaluate this using a cycle-accurate simulator (gem5) modified with SGX models, benchmarking against baseline SGX performance on standard cloud instances using real-world LSST precursor datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel hardware-software architecture blueprint for high-performance, secure cloud processing of scientific graphs. It will provide quantitative evidence of the performance gains possible with enclave-aware acceleration and release open-source simulator patches and kernel benchmarks to guide future secure cloud hardware design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored accelerators for graphs or secure enclaves in isolation, this proposal uniquely co-designs a domain-specific accelerator architecture specifically for the constraints and security guarantees of multi-tenant cloud server CPUs, targeting a concrete, data-intensive scientific use case (astronomy).

proposals.title: Kira-NVM: A Unified Memory Hierarchy for Persistent Astronomy Datasets Using Non-Volatile Memory and Caching Policies
proposals.motivation: Astronomy projects like the Vera C. Rubin Observatory's LSST will generate petabyte-scale, persistent datasets that are actively analyzed. Pre-2026, big data frameworks like Kira process such data across DRAM and disk, but the performance gap is immense, and DRAM is too costly for the entire working set. Emerging byte-addressable Non-Volatile Memory (NVM) technologies like Intel Optane DC Persistent Memory offer a new, large-capacity, slower-than-DRAM tier, but system software lacks intelligent, workload-aware hierarchies to integrate it seamlessly for persistent scientific workloads.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a cache-coherent, software-defined memory hierarchy, which explicitly manages NVM as a persistent, slowest-tier backing store for an application's entire working set, can deliver near-DRAM performance for iterative graph-based astronomy computations while dramatically reducing cost and simplifying data persistence.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira astronomy image processing framework with a new 'MemTier' library. This library will implement an application-directed, multi-tier cache manager for DRAM, NVM, and SSD. Using performance counters and memory access traces from real Kira graph workloads (e.g., source extraction, co-add), we will develop and tune new caching policies (beyond LRU) that optimize for NVM's asymmetric read/write latency and endurance. We will prototype the architecture on a server equipped with Intel Optane NVM, evaluating it against a baseline Kira deployment using only DRAM and disk.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a set of caching algorithms optimized for NVM-backed persistent scientific datasets, 2) an open-source software architecture for integrating NVM into big data processing frameworks like Apache Spark, as used by Kira, and 3) a quantitative performance and cost model for tiered memory systems in cloud-based astronomy pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores NVM as a fast disk or a slow DRAM, our approach is novel in treating NVM as the guaranteed-capacity, persistent backing store for an application's *entire* working set within a managed hierarchy, with caching policies informed by specific graph-based scientific compute patterns rather than generic server workloads.

proposals.title: RV128G: A RISC-V ISA Extension and Architectural Framework for 128-bit Address Spaces
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 trends in exascale computing, massive graph analytics (e.g., Kira's astronomical data), and in-memory databases are pushing beyond the practical limits of 64-bit virtual address spaces. Future systems with petabytes of DRAM, byte-addressable persistent memory, and disaggregated memory pools require a clean, scalable architectural solution to avoid the complexity and performance overheads of software-managed segmentation or multi-level paging schemes prevalent in pre-2026 systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That a carefully designed RV128G ISA extension, coupled with a tiered TLB and cache hierarchy, can provide a linear, efficient address space for massive memory workloads without imposing prohibitive overhead on common-case 64-bit operations or breaking existing software ecosystems.
proposals.method: We will first define the RV128G ISA extension, specifying new XLEN modes, instruction semantics for 128-bit pointer arithmetic, and a compatible page table structure. We will then develop a detailed microarchitectural model using a modified gem5 simulator, focusing on a three-tier TLB (L1: 64-bit hot, L2: hybrid, L3: sparse 128-bit) and cache tag organization. The design will be validated using trace-driven simulation from scaled-up versions of graph workloads (like those in Kira) and genomic assembly, comparing performance and overhead against 64-bit baseline with software overlays.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete, open-source specification and performance analysis of a 128-bit RISC-V architecture, directly informing future hardware designs for big data and cloud systems. It will establish clear guidelines for managing the transition between 64-bit and 128-bit address spaces within a single coherent framework.
proposals.novelty: While 128-bit addressing has been discussed theoretically, this proposal integrates it into the pragmatic, modular RISC-V ecosystem, focusing on the specific hardware mechanisms (like the tiered TLB) needed for practical efficiency, rather than treating it as a purely theoretical ISA exercise.

proposals.title: Kira-V: ISA and Coherence Extensions for Scalable Many-Core RISC-V SoCs for Data-Intensive Workloads
proposals.motivation: The rise of data-intensive applications in astronomy (like the Kira pipeline), genomics, and graph analytics demands scalable, energy-efficient many-core architectures. Pre-2026, RISC-V offers an open foundation for custom silicon, but its baseline ISA and coherence models lack the explicit primitives needed for efficient scaling beyond a few dozen cores in a tiled SoC, creating bottlenecks in data movement and synchronization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a set of focused RISC-V ISA extensions for explicit data movement and synchronization, combined with a configurable, hierarchical coherence model, will enable linear performance scaling on data-intensive workloads across tiled many-core (100+ core) RISC-V systems-on-chip.
proposals.method: We will design and simulate extensions using a modified gem5 simulator. First, we will define ISA primitives for bulk cache line transfer and producer-consumer signaling, inspired by concepts from the Tile64 and industry interconnect protocols. Second, we will design a configurable coherence protocol allowing tiles to operate in private, software-managed, or directory-based modes, optimized for workloads like graph traversal (Graph500) and genomic sequence alignment (BWA). Third, we will implement and evaluate these extensions on a simulated 256-core tiled architecture, comparing performance and energy against a baseline many-core RISC-V using standard cache-coherent protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a concrete, open-source proposal for RISC-V many-core scalability, including a set of ISA extensions and a coherence model specification. It will provide quantitative evidence of performance and efficiency gains for key data-parallel workloads, directly informing the design of future open-source hardware for cloud and specialized servers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work exists on many-core coherence, our approach uniquely integrates lightweight ISA extensions for dataflow with a *configurable* coherence model within the RISC-V ecosystem, targeting the specific data-movement patterns of Patterson's identified workloads (astronomy, genomics, graphs) rather than general-purpose computing.

proposals.title: Kira-Edge: Lightweight, In-Situ Log Analysis for Astronomy Sensor Networks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of edge devices in scientific fields like astronomy creates vast, distributed sensor networks generating operational logs. Centralized log analysis, as used in cloud-scale systems like Kira, is infeasible due to bandwidth constraints, latency, and cost. Pre-2026, there is a critical gap in methods for performing meaningful log summarization and anomaly detection directly on resource-constrained edge hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting the dataflow graph execution model of systems like Kira to a single-chip scale and employing novel, cache-aware log compression algorithms, we can enable real-time, in-situ log analysis on edge devices with minimal performance overhead and power consumption.
proposals.method: We will design a lightweight runtime, Kira-Edge, targeting RISC-V-based SoCs with constrained memory and cache hierarchies. The method involves: 1) Developing a static, ahead-of-time compiler that transforms log parsing rules into optimized, fixed dataflow graphs executable on a single core. 2) Creating a new 'selective Bloom filter' algorithm for real-time log entry deduplication and anomaly flagging, optimized for last-level cache residency. 3) Implementing a tiered summarization protocol that retains critical anomalies locally while transmitting only compact statistical digests, inspired by genomic sequence alignment heuristics for efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new systems architecture for edge log analytics, demonstrating a 10-100x reduction in data transmission for sensor networks. It will provide a concrete case study in scaling down big data methodologies, yielding publishable performance results on real astronomy sensor hardware and open-source RISC-V platforms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying Patterson's expertise in scalable system architecture (Kira, RISC) inversely—to the extreme miniaturization of analytics pipelines—and in the co-design of log-specific algorithms with constrained cache and memory architectures, a pre-2026 underexplored intersection.

proposals.title: Kira-D: A Distributed, Shared-Nothing Programming Model for Scalable Graph and Image Processing
proposals.motivation: The shared memory abstraction, while simplifying programming, is a fundamental bottleneck for scalability in modern data-intensive workloads like astronomy imagery (Kira) and large-scale graph analytics. As cloud and warehouse-scale computing dominate, the hardware reality is distributed, non-uniform memory and storage, making a global shared address space inefficient and limiting for fault tolerance and cost-effective scaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a disciplined, distributed dataflow programming model, which explicitly manages data placement and communication, can outperform and outscale shared-memory models for irregular, data-intensive applications without sacrificing programmer productivity.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira astronomy image processing framework into 'Kira-D', implementing a new backend based on Apache Spark's resilient distributed datasets (RDDs) for fault tolerance. We will design a domain-specific language (DSL) for graph and image operations that enforces a partitioned global address space (PGAS)-like view but compiles to a shared-nothing execution plan. We will evaluate against shared-memory systems (e.g., OpenMP on large NUMA) and existing big data stacks using real astronomy datasets and graph benchmarks like PageRank on cloud instances, measuring performance, scaling efficiency, and code complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, high-performance alternative programming model for Patterson's 'Process' theme, demonstrating how to build efficient systems without relying on a hardware-enforced shared memory illusion. It will contribute a new, open-source framework (Kira-D) for scalable scientific and graph computation and a set of design principles for 'shared-nothing-first' architectures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying Patterson's rigorous, industry-focused methodology for hardware and architecture to challenge a core software abstraction, creating a hybrid model that leverages big data system resilience while targeting the performance and low-overhead traditionally associated with HPC and custom hardware.

proposals.title: Kira-Tune: An Autotuning Framework for Astronomy and Genomic Pipelines Across Heterogeneous Cloud Instances
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of diverse cloud instance types (varying in core count, cache hierarchy, memory bandwidth, and attached accelerators) creates a significant performance portability challenge. Applications like the Kira astronomy pipeline and genomic analysis tools, which Patterson has explored, must be manually tuned for each target, wasting developer time and cloud credits. An automated solution is critical for cost-effective scientific computing in the cloud.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning-driven autotuner, guided by lightweight architectural signatures and application-specific performance models, can automatically generate near-optimal configuration parameters (e.g., thread count, tile size, algorithm variant) for a given application on any modern cloud instance, significantly reducing tuning effort while maximizing performance per dollar.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira framework to integrate an autotuning system. The method involves: 1) Instrumenting Kira and selected genomic tools to expose key tunable parameters (loop blocking, thread concurrency, I/O buffering). 2) Developing a profile-guided cost model that uses microbenchmarks to characterize an instance's memory hierarchy and core topology. 3) Implementing a Bayesian optimization search algorithm to efficiently explore the parameter space, using the cost model as a prior. Validation will be performed across diverse AWS (e.g., C5, M5, R5) and Google Cloud instance families.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical, open-source autotuning framework integrated with a major scientific pipeline (Kira), demonstrating measurable performance-per-dollar gains. It will provide a methodology for creating architecture-aware performance models for cloud workloads, moving beyond single-architecture tuning. The results will be presented as a case study for bringing industry-style autotuning to data-intensive scientific computing.
proposals.novelty: While autotuning exists for single architectures (e.g., ATLAS), our approach uniquely targets the dynamic, heterogeneous cloud environment, optimizing for a monetary cost metric rather than just raw performance. It combines Patterson's experience with big-data astronomy (Kira) and hardware characterization to build instance-specific models without requiring full hardware disclosure from cloud vendors.

proposals.title: Kira-FT: Fault-Tolerant Graph Processing Abstractions for Unreliable Cloud Hardware
proposals.motivation: The push for larger, more cost-effective cloud and data-center systems, using technologies like scale-out architectures and specialized accelerators, increases the probability of transient and permanent hardware faults during long-running computations. Existing parallel programming models (e.g., MapReduce, Spark, Pregel) often treat faults as catastrophic, relying on coarse-grained checkpoint/restart, which imposes high overhead for iterative, in-memory graph analytics like those in the Kira astronomy pipeline. A more efficient, inherent fault tolerance is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing new programming abstractions based on selective, algorithmic redundancy and lineage-based recovery at the sub-computation level, we can achieve inherent fault tolerance for iterative graph algorithms with significantly lower overhead than full-system checkpointing.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira/Big Data technology stack to create a new runtime and API. The approach integrates: 1) A novel 'Chunked Vertex-Centric' abstraction, where graph partitions are processed in redundant, overlapping chunks, allowing recovery from a lost chunk using neighbor data. 2) Lightweight, deterministic lineage logging for state updates within an iteration, enabling replay from a prior iteration's checkpoint. 3) Hardware fault detection leveraging existing ECC, cache coherence protocols, and performance counter anomalies to trigger recovery mechanisms transparently.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, open-source extension to the Kira framework providing fault-tolerant graph processing abstractions. It will contribute a quantitative analysis of the performance-fault tolerance trade-off for real-world astronomy and social network graphs, providing a blueprint for embedding fault tolerance into future domain-specific architectures.
proposals.novelty: Unlike coarse-grained checkpointing or triple-modular redundancy, our method integrates fault tolerance directly into the graph computation model via algorithmic redundancy and fine-grained lineage, a departure from system-level solutions. It specifically targets the unreliable hardware trends Patterson has highlighted, applying a 'hardware/software codesign' philosophy to fault management.

proposals.title: Kira-ML: Machine Learning for Dynamic Runtime Decisions in Large-Scale Astronomy Image Processing
proposals.motivation: The Kira framework for astronomy imagery demonstrates the challenge of managing irregular, data-intensive graph-parallel workloads on cloud hardware. Static scheduling and data placement strategies, common in pre-2026 systems, lead to suboptimal performance and resource utilization due to unpredictable data access patterns and load imbalance. This inefficiency directly impacts the cost and scientific throughput of large-scale sky surveys.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lightweight, online machine learning models can predict task execution times and data locality patterns, enabling a runtime system to make near-optimal decisions for dynamic scheduling, load balancing, and cache-conscious data placement, thereby significantly improving end-to-end performance.
proposals.method: We will extend the Kira architecture with a dedicated ML co-processor module. This module will use online reinforcement learning (e.g., a contextual bandits algorithm) to adapt scheduling policies based on real-time performance counters and graph topology features. For data placement, we will train gradient-boosted decision tree models (XGBoost) on historical memory access traces to predict hot data subsets, guiding prefetching and NUMA-aware allocation. The system will be evaluated on cloud instances with heterogeneous cores and deep cache hierarchies, processing real Sloan Digital Sky Survey graph workloads.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a production-grade, ML-augmented runtime for the Kira framework, 2) a set of learned models and policies for astronomy graph processing, and 3) a quantitative analysis of the trade-offs between ML inference overhead and performance gains in a commercial cloud setting. It will provide a blueprint for integrating predictive ML into system software.
proposals.novelty: While ML for static compiler optimizations is studied, its application to *dynamic*, fine-grained runtime decisions within a production big data system like Kira is novel. The tight co-design of ML models with the specific hardware (cache, chip) and workload (astronomical graph) characteristics moves beyond generic schedulers.

proposals.title: Kira-Ex: A Hierarchical Task-Graph Programming Model for Exascale Astronomy Image Processing
proposals.motivation: The push towards exascale computing, with systems comprising millions of heterogeneous cores and deep, non-uniform memory hierarchies, renders traditional parallel programming models (e.g., MPI+X) untenable due to overhead and complexity. This is acutely felt in data-intensive fields like astronomy, where projects like the LSST generate petabyte-scale image datasets requiring irregular, graph-like processing pipelines, as demonstrated by the pre-2026 Kira framework. A new model is needed to efficiently express and manage extreme concurrency across these complex hardware stacks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical, locality-aware task-graph programming model, co-designed with a lightweight, hardware-informed runtime, can efficiently harness systems with millions of threads by explicitly managing data movement across deep memory hierarchies, thereby dramatically improving performance and programmability for irregular data analytics workloads.
proposals.method: We will extend the dataflow principles of the Kira astronomy image processing pipeline to create 'Kira-Ex', a new programming model and runtime. The model will allow programmers to define composable task graphs with explicit data locality hints (e.g., cache, DRAM, NVRAM). The runtime will use a distributed, hierarchical scheduler inspired by prior work on warehouse-scale computers, employing performance counters and hardware telemetry to dynamically map tasks and data to cores, caches, and memory tiers. We will prototype and evaluate Kira-Ex using simulation (e.g., gem5) and emulation on a large-scale cloud cluster, targeting real astronomy workloads and synthetic graph benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel programming abstraction for extreme-concurrency systems that bridges the gap between irregular algorithms (like graph processing) and heterogeneous hardware, 2) an open-source runtime system that demonstrates scalable management of million-thread task graphs, and 3) a detailed performance analysis of astronomy imaging pipelines on exascale-like hardware, providing a blueprint for other data-intensive domains.
proposals.novelty: While task-based models exist, Kira-Ex's novelty lies in its tight integration of domain-specific dataflow (from astronomy imagery processing) with a hardware-centric runtime that proactively manages the entire deep memory hierarchy (cache to disk) as a first-class programming construct, moving beyond simple thread pooling.

proposals.title: Kira-Edge: A Hardware-Aware, Lightweight Pipeline for Portable Astronomy and Genomic Imaging Scanners
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of portable, point-of-care imaging devices in fields like astronomy (e.g., portable radio telescopes) and genomics (e.g., portable DNA sequencers) creates a need for on-device, real-time data processing. However, existing big data pipelines like Kira, designed for cloud-scale clusters, are too resource-intensive for the constrained memory, compute, and power budgets of edge hardware. This gap hinders rapid analysis and decision-making in remote or resource-limited settings.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying principles of hardware/software co-design and selective algorithm approximation, a significantly scaled-down version of the Kira image processing pipeline can be created that runs efficiently on low-power, cache-constrained System-on-Chip (SoC) architectures without sacrificing critical diagnostic or scientific accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop Kira-Edge by first profiling the standard Kira astronomy pipeline to identify computational bottlenecks and memory access patterns. We will then design a hardware-aware software architecture that employs (1) aggressive data pruning and tile-based processing to fit within small, hierarchical cache structures, (2) approximate computing kernels (e.g., for convolution and source detection) that trade configurable precision for latency and energy savings, and (3) a just-in-time compilation strategy targeting heterogeneous cores (ARM CPUs, embedded GPUs) common in portable scanners. The pipeline will be validated on a prototype using a Raspberry Pi-class board with a custom imaging sensor interface.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, open-source edge-optimized version of the Kira pipeline, enabling real-time image analysis on portable scanners. It will contribute a methodological framework for 'scaling down' big data workflows to embedded systems, with quantified trade-offs between accuracy, performance, and energy consumption. This directly advances the field of edge computing for scientific instrumentation.
proposals.novelty: While edge computing is well-studied for IoT and vision tasks, applying hardware/software co-design principles from high-performance architecture to scale a specific, complex astronomy/imaging big data pipeline (Kira) for portable scientific hardware is novel. The integration of configurable approximation techniques within a domain-specific, tile-based processing model tailored for small caches is a distinct approach.

ideas.title: Cost-Aware Data Lineage for Optimizing Cloud Storage and Transfer
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework that integrates fine-grained data lineage from scientific workflows with a cost model that treats data movement and persistence as the primary economic constraints in cloud data pipelines. By annotating lineage graphs with actual or predicted costs for storage classes, network egress, and inter-region transfers, the system enables cost attribution, auditing, and 'what-if' analysis for pipeline redesign. This allows engineers to debug not just for correctness, but for expense, identifying and eliminating high-cost, low-value data materializations and transfers. The result is a proactive optimization tool that uses lineage to make storage and transfer costs tangible, actionable, and central to data pipeline architecture.

ideas.title: Create scalable cloud storage benchmarks
ideas.description: Design new benchmarks to evaluate the cost-performance trade-offs of cloud storage services for varied applications.
ideas.sourceTitle: Above the Clouds: A Berkeley View of Cloud Computing

ideas.title: Scale up: programming exascale systems with extreme concurrency
ideas.description: Design models for systems with millions of threads and deep memory hierarchies.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Landscape of Parallel Computing Research: A View from Berkeley

ideas.title: Data Transfer as the 'Dark Energy' of Distributed Computing
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that computation is the primary cost driver in computing systems, focusing instead on data transfer and storage expenses. It uses an analogy from astrophysics and cosmology, specifically the ΛCDM model, which revealed that the universe's dynamics are dominated by dark energy and dark matter, not visible matter. The analogous solution was a paradigm shift in measuring the universe's mass-energy budget, focusing on the dominant but less obvious components. Adapted to computing, this leads to the 'Data-Transfer-Dark-Matter' (DTDM) framework. The research would develop new cost models and benchmarks that treat data movement (akin to dark energy) and data persistence/state (akin to dark matter) as the primary, often systemic, costs in distributed and high-performance computing, fundamentally re-framing architectural and economic analyses away from a compute-centric view.

ideas.title: Capability-Aware Scheduling and Abstraction for Heterogeneous Cloud Infrastructure
ideas.description: This research designs a cloud management system that makes heterogeneous hardware (GPUs, FPGAs, specialized ASICs) efficiently usable without burdening developers. It introduces a declarative API where users specify workload intents and constraints (e.g., 'train this model under 2 hours for < $200'), not specific hardware. The system's scheduler uses a multi-armed bandit and cost-performance model to dynamically and optimally map these intents to underlying heterogeneous resources, learning from telemetry to improve placements over time. The result is a cloud that leverages hardware diversity for performance and cost benefits while maintaining a simple, predictable developer experience.

ideas.title: Improve data-intensive computing cloud frameworks
ideas.description: Enhance programming frameworks like MapReduce for better efficiency in data-intensive computing on cloud infrastructure.
ideas.sourceTitle: Above the Clouds: A Berkeley View of Cloud Computing

ideas.title: Scale up to global desktop grid computing
ideas.description: Extend the NOW concept to a global-scale, internet-wide desktop grid for parallelism.
ideas.sourceTitle: A case for NOW (networks of workstation)

ideas.title: Scale up: LogP for extreme-scale exascale systems
ideas.description: Study how LogP parameters and algorithms must evolve for machines with millions of processors.
ideas.sourceTitle: LogP: towards a realistic model of parallel computation

ideas.title: Enable new cloud programming models
ideas.description: Invent programming models that simplify application development for scalable cloud infrastructure.
ideas.sourceTitle: A view of cloud computing

ideas.title: Scale up to cloud server CPUs
ideas.description: Adapt the custom co-processor concept for high-performance, multi-core server CPUs to protect cloud enclaves.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Rocket Chip Generator

ideas.title: Micro-clouds for edge and personal computing
ideas.description: Scale down cloud architecture to personal or edge micro-clouds for low-latency, private data processing.
ideas.sourceTitle: Above the Clouds: A Berkeley View of Cloud Computing

ideas.title: Bounded-Intensity Serverless: Predictable, Millisecond-Scale Task Orchestration
ideas.description: This research proposes a 'Bounded-Intensity Serverless' model, which combines a design philosophy of predictable, bounded workloads with an execution model optimized for millisecond-scale tasks. The core innovation is a scheduling and resource provisioning system that treats its finite capacity as a first-class constraint, enabling strong guarantees on tail latency, inter-task communication, and state access for ephemeral functions. This allows the reliable orchestration of complex, time-sensitive task graphs—such as those found in embedded edge analytics or high-frequency trading logic—within a serverless abstraction. The result is a platform suitable for deterministic soft real-time workloads that must complete within a known, stable performance envelope, fundamentally redefining serverless as a predictable compute fabric rather than a scale-out utility.

ideas.title: Middleware for Dynamic Orchestration of Heterogeneous Personal Compute Fabrics
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel middleware architecture, inspired by enterprise service buses and publish-subscribe models, designed specifically for the dynamic, ad-hoc integration of a user's personally owned heterogeneous hardware devices (CPUs, GPUs, NPUs, FPGAs). The system would abstract these diverse resources into a unified 'personal compute fabric,' featuring a smart broker capable of matching computational tasks—such as AI inference, media processing, or scientific simulation—to the most suitable available specialized hardware based on capability, power, and latency. This enables automatic, context-aware offloading and parallel execution across a user's device ecosystem, transforming a collection of disparate gadgets into a powerful, self-optimizing, and personal heterogeneous cloud.

ideas.title: Scale out to many-core tiled architectures
ideas.description: Define ISA extensions and coherence models for scalable many-core RISC-V systems-on-chip.
ideas.sourceTitle: The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual

ideas.title: Create automated performance prediction tools
ideas.description: Build compilers or tools that use LogP parameters to automatically predict and optimize parallel code performance.
ideas.sourceTitle: LogP: towards a realistic model of parallel computation

ideas.title: Federated Heterogeneous Cloud Fabrics for Dynamic Vertical Specialization
ideas.description: This research explores the architecture and orchestration for federating multiple cloud providers, each offering heterogeneous hardware (GPUs, FPGAs, TPUs, etc.), into a single logical resource fabric. The core challenge is to enable seamless vertical scaling where an application can dynamically request not just more compute, but a specific, optimized blend of specialized resources drawn from across the federation. This moves beyond simple resource aggregation to enable intelligent, workload-aware composition of a 'best-of-breed' mega-cloud, optimizing for performance, cost, and energy efficiency simultaneously. Key research directions include cross-provider abstraction models for heterogeneity, federated scheduling for accelerator-aware placement, and new economic and security models for trading specialized capacity.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Just-in-Time Compilation for Cloud Data Frameworks Inspired by Metabolic Pathway Regulation
ideas.description: This research seed proposes enhancing programming frameworks like MapReduce for better efficiency in data-intensive cloud computing by drawing an analogy from systems biology. In cellular metabolism, pathways are dynamically regulated via feedback mechanisms to optimize resource use based on current demands. The analogous solution is to adapt this principle by designing a cloud data framework with a runtime profiling and just-in-time compilation layer. This layer would continuously monitor data characteristics and cluster state, enabling it to dynamically reconfigure the execution graph—for instance, by fusing map and reduce stages to avoid expensive shuffling when intermediate data is small, or by splitting tasks differently when skew is detected. The result is an adaptive framework that moves beyond static planning to achieve significantly better resource efficiency and job completion times in volatile cloud environments.

ideas.title: Design fault-tolerant parallel programming abstractions
ideas.description: Create programming abstractions that inherently tolerate hardware faults in large-scale systems.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Landscape of Parallel Computing Research: A View from Berkeley

ideas.title: A Heterogeneous Cloud Resource Orchestrator Inspired by Metabolic Pathways
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of homogeneous hardware infrastructure by proposing a novel orchestration framework for clouds with specialized, heterogeneous hardware (e.g., GPUs, FPGAs, TPUs). The analogy is drawn from Systems Biology, specifically metabolic pathway analysis, where cells efficiently process diverse substrates using a network of specialized enzymes. The analogous solution involves modeling and optimizing flux through these heterogeneous catalytic networks. Adapted to cloud computing, the research would develop a 'Metabolic Resource Orchestrator.' This system would treat computational tasks as substrates and heterogeneous hardware units as specialized catalysts. It would create a dynamic, graph-based model of the cloud's hardware capabilities and continuously map incoming workloads onto the most efficient 'pathway' through this heterogeneous resource network, optimizing for performance, energy efficiency, or cost, much like a cell optimizes metabolic flux for growth or production.

ideas.title: Create cloud computing benchmarks
ideas.description: Establish benchmarks to measure and compare performance across cloud services.
ideas.sourceTitle: A view of cloud computing

ideas.title: Scale down: Micro-datacenters in a single package
ideas.description: Design a single chip that integrates the full heterogeneity and hierarchy of an entire warehouse-scale computer.
ideas.sourceTitle: Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach

ideas.title: Autotune applications for diverse parallel architectures
ideas.description: Build autotuning systems to adapt applications to varying core counts and memory hierarchies.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Landscape of Parallel Computing Research: A View from Berkeley

ideas.title: Temporally-Aware Data-Intensive Computing for Multi-Modal Lifelog Analytics
ideas.description: This research fuses cloud-scale data processing frameworks with multimedia temporal alignment techniques to create a new paradigm for analyzing heterogeneous, high-volume lifelog data. It proposes extending frameworks like MapReduce or Spark with native operators for aligning and correlating asynchronous data streams (e.g., video, audio, biometrics, location) based on temporal semantics, not just key-value pairs. This allows complex, time-sensitive analytics across petabytes of personal data to be expressed efficiently as a single distributed job, moving alignment from a costly pre-processing step into the core parallel execution engine. The result is a cloud-optimized system that can derive contextual insights from temporally misaligned lifelogs at a scale and latency previously unattainable by either domain alone.

ideas.title: Apply data lineage from scientific workflows
ideas.description: Adapt data provenance and lineage techniques from scientific computing to audit and debug cloud data pipelines.
ideas.sourceTitle: Above the Clouds: A Berkeley View of Cloud Computing

ideas.title: Capillary-Scale Distributed Computing with Autonomous Resource Arbitration
ideas.description: This research expands the micro-cloud concept by applying an analogy from human circulatory physiology. The core problem of scaling down cloud architecture for low-latency, private edge computing parallels the physiological challenge of distributing resources efficiently to vast numbers of independent cells. The analogous solution is the autonomous, hierarchical, and demand-regulated capillary network. The adapted idea proposes a 'capillary-scale' computing architecture where personal or edge micro-clouds autonomously arbitrate resources. They employ a bio-inspired dual-priority protocol for task routing and local policy 'sphincters' to control data and compute flow. This creates a resilient, decentralized fabric for edge processing that minimizes latency by keeping data local when possible, but can efficiently tap neighboring resources during high demand, all without centralized control, thereby enhancing both performance and data privacy.

ideas.title: Assume computation is the primary cost
ideas.description: Challenge that computation is the main cost driver, focusing instead on data transfer and storage expenses.
ideas.sourceTitle: Above the Clouds: A Berkeley View of Cloud Computing

ideas.title: Scale down: Lightweight log analysis for edge devices
ideas.description: Adapt the methodology for resource-constrained environments with limited log volume and compute.

ideas.title: Scale memory hierarchy for persistent datasets
ideas.description: Design hierarchies where the largest, slowest tier is non-volatile and holds the working set.
ideas.sourceTitle: Computer Architecture, Fifth Edition: A Quantitative Approach

ideas.title: Intent-Based Opportunistic Fabric for Personal-Edge Computing
ideas.description: This system enables low-latency, private data processing by dynamically composing compute and storage from a user's own nearby devices (phones, laptops, IoT) and trusted edge resources. Instead of deploying fixed micro-clouds, it uses a declarative API where applications specify performance, privacy, and data locality constraints. A lightweight, decentralized scheduler then partitions and places tasks as ephemeral functions across the available fabric, handling discovery, orchestration, and state management without requiring a persistent, complex software stack on every participating device.

ideas.title: Dual: Use NOW to simulate a supercomputer
ideas.description: Use a large NOW to emulate the behavior and performance of a tightly-coupled supercomputer.
ideas.sourceTitle: A case for NOW (networks of workstation)

ideas.title: Challenge the shared memory abstraction necessity
ideas.description: Explore efficient programming models that do not rely on a global shared memory.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Landscape of Parallel Computing Research: A View from Berkeley

ideas.title: Relaxing the need for hardware specialization
ideas.description: Investigate general-purpose architectures that match specialized hardware efficiency through novel compilation or runtime techniques.
ideas.sourceTitle: Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach

ideas.title: Unified Cost-Optimization via Dynamic Computation-Data Trade-off Modeling
ideas.description: This research proposes a holistic cost-optimization framework that moves beyond arguing for a single primary cost driver. It develops a fine-grained, adaptable cost model that captures the complex interdependencies between computation, data transfer, and storage expenses in cloud and distributed environments. The core of the system is an intelligent orchestrator that uses this model to make continuous, optimal decisions about data locality, computational redundancy, and caching. This enables automatic adaptation to fluctuating resource prices and workload patterns, minimizing total system cost without a priori bias toward any single expense category.

ideas.title: Use machine learning for parallel runtime decisions
ideas.description: Apply ML to dynamically manage scheduling, load balancing, and data placement.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Landscape of Parallel Computing Research: A View from Berkeley

ideas.title: Challenge low communication requirement for parallelism
ideas.description: Explore if NOWs can efficiently solve problems requiring moderate inter-task communication.
ideas.sourceTitle: A case for NOW (networks of workstation)

ideas.title: Inverse problem: cloud to edge offloading
ideas.description: Study offloading computation from the cloud back to edge or client devices.
ideas.sourceTitle: A view of cloud computing

ideas.title: Heterogeneous Dual Optimization: Minimizing Cost for Fixed Performance on Specialized Hardware
ideas.description: This research proposes a dual optimization framework for cloud workloads where a strict performance target (e.g., latency, throughput) must be met at minimum financial cost by selecting from a heterogeneous pool of specialized hardware instances. It moves beyond simple instance sizing to solve the core challenge of architectural selection, determining whether a workload's target is best met by a GPU, FPGA, CPU cluster, or other accelerator, based on a model of performance and cost per architecture. The solution requires novel performance prediction and cost modeling that accounts for the distinct performance profiles and pricing models of heterogeneous hardware, ultimately generating optimal, cost-minimizing assignments that are impossible under a homogeneity assumption.

ideas.title: Proximal Serverless: Millisecond Task Execution via Pre-Provisioned Edge Contexts
ideas.description: This system enables true millisecond-scale serverless execution by eliminating the dominant network and cold-start overheads. It pre-deploys a managed pool of secure, minimal runtime contexts (e.g., for Python, JavaScript, WebAssembly) on edge nodes, client-adjacent servers, or even within the client framework itself. A lightweight local dispatcher matches incoming micro-tasks to an already-warm, compatible context, enabling sub-millisecond invocation via local IPC or shared memory. The model maintains serverless semantics—the user only submits the task and data—while the system transparently decides whether to execute it proximally or route longer tasks to the traditional cloud backend.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Byzantine Fault Tolerance in Cloud Storage: A Framework for Quantifying Resilience-Performance-Cost Trade-offs
ideas.description: This research synthesizes distributed systems resilience and benchmarking to create a new framework for evaluating cloud storage services. It designs specialized benchmarks that inject Byzantine faults (e.g., malicious or arbitrary node behavior) and measure the resulting impact on latency, throughput, and operational cost across different storage configurations and consensus protocols (like PBFT, HotStuff, or Federated Byzantine Agreement). The outcome is a standardized methodology to empirically quantify the trade-offs between security guarantees, performance, and cost, providing cloud architects and developers with actionable data to select optimal fault-tolerance strategies for their specific reliability and budget requirements.

ideas.title: Scale up to 128-bit address space
ideas.description: Design a RV128G architecture for future massive memory and address space needs.
ideas.sourceTitle: The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual

ideas.title: Bimodal System Architecture for Predictable Baselines with Managed Burst Regimes
ideas.description: This research investigates system architectures designed for a dual-regime workload model: a highly optimized, predictable core for the common baseline, and a separate, provisioned burst layer for handling excess load. The core contribution is a formal framework for evaluating the trade-offs—in latency, cost, consistency, and complexity—between these two tiers, which may employ entirely different data structures, consistency models, and hardware. The goal is to provide principled design guidelines for systems where infinite scalability is not assumed, but where pure bounded-optimization is also insufficient, enabling robust performance within realistic, variable operating envelopes.

ideas.title: Generative and Adaptive Benchmarking for Cloud Storage Cost-Performance
ideas.description: This research designs a generative benchmarking system that creates dynamic, stateful workload traces modeled after specific application archetypes (e.g., CI/CD pipelines, data lakes, microservices). The system incorporates a feedback-driven adaptation engine that continuously refines benchmark parameters and cost models based on prior results and changes in cloud provider pricing and performance. This approach moves beyond static tests to produce predictive, application-specific cost-performance trade-off analyses that remain relevant over time. The outcome is an open-source framework that allows practitioners to generate bespoke benchmarks tailored to their operational patterns.

ideas.title: Scale-down serverless for brief tasks
ideas.description: Extreme scale-down: a serverless model optimized for millisecond-scale tasks, not just function execution.
ideas.sourceTitle: Above the Clouds: A Berkeley View of Cloud Computing

ideas.title: Scale up mobile SoC to cloud-in-a-box

ideas.title: Scale down for edge computing on portable scanners
ideas.description: Create lightweight, optimized pipeline versions for execution on limited hardware at point-of-care imaging sites.
ideas.sourceTitle: Toil enables reproducible, open source, big biomedical data analyses

ideas.title: Incorporate memory hierarchy and caching
ideas.description: Introduce parameters for local cache effects and memory access costs, which LogP omits.
ideas.sourceTitle: LogP: towards a realistic model of parallel computation

ideas.title: Federated Personal Data Clouds for Scalable, Privacy-Preserving Lifelogging
ideas.description: This research addresses the practical challenges of multimodal lifelogging—such as massive data volume, real-time processing needs, and siloed service integration—by leveraging a federated cloud architecture scaled vertically for the individual user. Instead of relying on a single provider, the system dynamically allocates different lifelog data streams (video, audio, biometrics) and processing tasks across a secure, user-controlled federation of cloud resources, creating a 'personal mega-cloud'. This enables previously infeasible, large-scale longitudinal analysis and AI model personalization on the complete lifelog dataset without centralizing the data, fundamentally shifting the paradigm from application-centric logging to a scalable, private, user-centric data infrastructure.

ideas.title: Intent-Based Compilation and Policy Enforcement for Multi-Cloud Portability
ideas.description: This system treats cloud providers' unique services as a feature, not a bug, by developing a compiler that translates a standardized, high-level declarative specification of an application's needs into optimized, provider-specific deployments. A continuously updated knowledge graph models the capabilities, performance, and cost structures of each provider's services. The compiler uses this graph, along with developer-defined portability policies, to select and configure the best-fit services across clouds while embedding instrumentation and automation to maintain portability, such as data egress pathways and fallback deployment blueprints, ensuring the application is not trapped even while leveraging differentiated services.

ideas.title: Assume intermittent, not continuous, connectivity
ideas.description: Design mobile and cloud architectures optimized for operation during frequent network partitions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach, 2nd Edition

ideas.title: Invert the data center location assumption
ideas.description: Study clouds built from highly distributed, micro-scale compute nodes, not centralized data centers.
ideas.sourceTitle: A view of cloud computing

ideas.title: Queueing-Theoretic Active Curation for Predictive Cloud Performance
ideas.description: This research develops a framework where users in a multi-tenant cloud environment actively curate and tag their tasks with semantic importance, deadlines, and performance sensitivity. This curated intent data is fed as a primary input into advanced, multi-class queueing network models. The system then predicts performance bottlenecks and dynamically optimizes scheduling, prioritization, and resource allocation not just based on system metrics, but explicitly aligned with user-declared intent and business value. This shifts cloud performance management from reactive, system-centric scaling to proactive, value-aware orchestration.

ideas.title: Scale down to micro-service granularity
ideas.description: Investigate the performance and management limits of extremely fine-grained cloud services.
ideas.sourceTitle: A view of cloud computing

ideas.title: Co-design architectures for power and dependability
ideas.description: Jointly optimize architecture for power efficiency and system dependability in mobile and cloud contexts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach, 2nd Edition

ideas.title: Semantic Resource Federation: Predictive Orchestration for Hybrid Infrastructure
ideas.description: This research develops a predictive orchestration system that creates a semantically-aware federation between owned infrastructure and cloud resources. It addresses the fundamental mismatch problem by continuously profiling workload patterns and resource characteristics to build predictive models of performance and cost equivalency. The system employs proactive resource alignment, including data pre-positioning and compute pre-warming, to eliminate latency and bottleneck issues during bursts. This enables truly seamless workload migration across heterogeneous environments while maintaining predictable performance and cost controls through isomorphic abstraction layers.

ideas.title: Dual: Minimizing cost for fixed performance
ideas.description: Solve the dual problem: given a fixed performance target, minimize cost via optimal cloud resource selection.
ideas.sourceTitle: Above the Clouds: A Berkeley View of Cloud Computing

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Designing for worst-case, not average-case
ideas.description: Optimize mobile-cloud architectures for guaranteed worst-case performance bounds, not average throughput.
ideas.sourceTitle: Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach

ideas.title: Synaptic Serverless: Event-Driven Micro-Task Execution Inspired by Neural Transmission
ideas.description: This research proposes 'Synaptic Serverless,' a novel architecture for millisecond-scale tasks inspired by efficiency mechanisms in neural transmission. Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience, where synapses perform rapid, localized signal processing using pooled vesicles and precise reuptake, we adapt this to cloud computing. The solution involves designing a serverless model that maintains pools of pre-initialized, minimal execution contexts at the network edge. A central controller dispatches micro-tasks to these contexts, which execute in milliseconds and then immediately release all resources—akin to neurotransmitter release and reuptake—enabling extreme scale-down and near-zero cold start latency for brief, event-driven tasks, far beyond the granularity of current function-as-a-service platforms.

ideas.title: Assume energy is the primary constraint, not transistors
ideas.description: Design parallel computing models where energy efficiency dominates performance optimization.

ideas.title: Apply scheduling from operating systems

ideas.title: Create performance models for NOW task scheduling
ideas.description: Build predictive models for task scheduling performance in heterogeneous NOW environments.

ideas.title: Privacy-Preserving Inverse Bursting: Oblivious Computation on Owned Infrastructure
ideas.description: This research designs a hybrid computing architecture that inverts the typical cloud-centric model by treating owned, on-premises infrastructure as the primary, trusted execution environment. To handle workload spikes, the system 'bursts' specific, privacy-sensitive computational tasks to public cloud resources, but does so by transforming the tasks—using cryptographic or algorithmic techniques—so the cloud provider remains functionally unaware of the actual data and computation being performed. This approach synthesizes the cost-control and data sovereignty of inverse cloud-bursting with the strong privacy guarantees of device unawareness, enabling confidential, scalable hybrid processing for sensitive domains like healthcare and finance.

ideas.title: Scale to protect entire operating system
ideas.description: Integrate the diversification framework at the OS level to protect system calls and kernel operations.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Rocket Chip Generator

ideas.title: Invert the focus from performance to correctness
ideas.description: Prioritize verifiable correctness in parallel models over raw performance gains.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Landscape of Parallel Computing Research: A View from Berkeley

ideas.title: Auction-Based Federated Micro-Clouds for Dynamic Edge Resource Markets
ideas.description: This research synthesizes micro-clouds and market design to create a federated edge computing network where individual micro-cloud nodes (e.g., in homes, vehicles, or small businesses) can participate in a decentralized resource market. Using auction theory and market-based protocols, these nodes dynamically bid to sell their idle compute cycles, storage, or specialized hardware (like GPUs) and to buy resources from neighboring nodes for low-latency task offloading or data processing. This enables a robust, privacy-preserving edge infrastructure that automatically optimizes resource allocation based on real-time economic signals, overcoming the static provisioning of traditional clouds and the isolation of standalone micro-clouds.

ideas.title: Federated clouds for vertical scaling
ideas.description: Scale up by federating multiple cloud providers to create a seamless, vertically-scaled mega-cloud resource pool.
ideas.sourceTitle: Above the Clouds: A Berkeley View of Cloud Computing

ideas.title: Queueing-Theoretic Modeling for Performance and Utility Optimization in Single-Modality Lifelog Pipelines
ideas.description: This research applies advanced queueing network models to the specific, constrained domain of single-device lifelogging. It treats the continuous stream of data and its processing stages—capture, compression, feature extraction, and inference—as a queueing system with finite buffers and variable service rates. The model is used to analytically predict bottlenecks and latency, but uniquely links these performance metrics to the utility of the lifelog analysis, formalizing trade-offs between processing speed, resource cost, and the fidelity or timeliness of derived insights. This enables the design of adaptive systems that dynamically tune pipeline parameters to maximize personal utility under resource constraints, a problem not addressed by either queueing theory or lifelog analysis in isolation.

ideas.title: Architectures for extreme energy-proportional mobile devices
ideas.description: Design hardware that scales performance and power consumption more efficiently for the intermittent workloads of mobile clients.
ideas.sourceTitle: Computer Architecture, Fifth Edition: A Quantitative Approach

ideas.title: Develop standardized metrics for pipeline comparison
ideas.description: Create benchmarks and metrics to quantitatively compare different processing pipelines within the O3D framework.
ideas.sourceTitle: Toil enables reproducible, open source, big biomedical data analyses

ideas.title: Transfer co-design from HPC
ideas.description: Use hardware-software co-design techniques to tailor RISC-V extensions for specific algorithms.
ideas.sourceTitle: The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual

ideas.title: Apply database concurrency control to parallel tasks
ideas.description: Use serializability and isolation techniques from databases for parallel task consistency.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Landscape of Parallel Computing Research: A View from Berkeley

ideas.title: Inverse: Cloud-bursting to owned infrastructure
ideas.description: Study the inverse of cloud-bursting, using owned infrastructure as the primary resource and bursting to the cloud.
ideas.sourceTitle: Above the Clouds: A Berkeley View of Cloud Computing

ideas.title: Decentralized Gossip-Based Middleware for Ad-Hoc Personal Device Integration
ideas.description: This research designs a lightweight middleware system for personal devices that abandons the centralized broker model. Instead, it employs a peer-to-peer overlay network where devices use gossip protocols to discover nearby services and share state information. Communication is direct between devices, eliminating the single point of failure and performance bottleneck. The system prioritizes loose coupling, fault tolerance, and operation in dynamic, resource-constrained networks typical of personal computing environments.

ideas.title: Inverse Cloud-Bursting: A Demand-Side Grid Management Model for Compute
ideas.description: This research studies the inverse of cloud-bursting by applying the principles of demand-side management from electrical power grids. In that analogous field, distributed, owned generation (e.g., solar panels) is prioritized, with the centralized grid used as a supplemental burst resource, guided by smart meters and dynamic pricing. The adapted idea treats owned compute infrastructure as the primary, managed resource and the public cloud as the supplemental utility. The research will develop a framework involving predictive workload analysis, real-time economic signals, and intelligent schedulers to automatically prioritize execution on owned hardware, bursting to the cloud only during genuine capacity shortfalls or when spot-market pricing makes it more cost-effective than running owned assets. This flips the traditional cloud-centric scaling model to an ownership-first paradigm.

ideas.title: Bandwidth-Aware Lifelogging: Engineering for Expressive, Cost-Constrained Personal Archives
ideas.description: This research formalizes software engineering principles for building personal lifelogging systems that must operate under the core constraint of expensive and highly variable bandwidth. It moves beyond simple compression or caching to develop architectures where data capture granularity, encoding, synchronization strategy, and even the choice of sensors are dynamically influenced by real-time and predicted network costs. The resulting guide provides patterns for creating 'expressive' lifelogs that maximize informational value per bit transmitted, enabling practical, sustainable systems for continuous personal data integration in bandwidth-constrained or metered environments worldwide.

ideas.title: Designing a 'Nutritional Label' for Cloud Storage: A Benchmark Suite Based on Application Metabolic Profiles
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from nutritional science, where standardized labels solve the problem of comparing diverse foods for varied health goals, to the problem of evaluating diverse cloud storage services for varied applications. The analogous solution is the creation of a consistent nutritional label quantifying key components. Adapted to cloud storage, the idea is to design a new benchmark suite that defines a set of core, application-centric 'metabolic' metrics—such as I/O intensity, latency tolerance, data durability requirements, and access pattern randomness. Running this benchmark on a cloud storage service would generate a clear, standardized 'Storage Nutritional Label.' This label would enable direct, scalable comparison of cost-performance trade-offs by allowing developers to match a service's quantified profile to the precise 'metabolic' signature of their own application workload, moving beyond one-size-fits-all benchmarks.

ideas.title: Assume Intermittent and Opportunistic Bandwidth
ideas.description: This research challenges the core assumption of persistent connectivity by designing systems for environments where bandwidth is not just expensive or variable, but frequently absent. It proposes a new architectural paradigm where applications and protocols are built around opportunistic data synchronization and local computation during disconnection. The system uses predictive models and context-aware prefetching to maximize utility when a fleeting connection is available, ensuring progress even in severely constrained or hostile network conditions. This approach is fundamental for robust operation in remote sensing, rural computing, and crisis response scenarios.
